The deal turns a former bitcoin miner into a landlord for AI training capacity, a sign of how much of the buildout is now happening off the labs' own balance sheets. As Anthropic locks in multi-year compute at this scale, the constraint on frontier AI is shifting from model research to power, land, and the financing needed to secure both.

title: Google backs nuclear fusion startup targeting Europe’s first commercial power plant
blurb: Google backed Germany's Proxima Fusion in a €411M ($468M) round that values the stellarator startup at $2.7B, making it Europe's best-funded fusion company by a wide margin. The strategic bet is on fusion as long-term abundant, carbon-free, firm power, with a proof-of-concept demonstrator targeted for the early 2030s and a commercial plant later that decade. The gap with the US is still large, as Commonwealth Fusion has raised $2.9B total and Helion $1.5B, so Europe is closing distance without yet catching up.

title: Klarna applies for US banking licence
blurb: Klarna applied to the Utah Department of Financial Institutions and the FDIC to charter Klarna Bank in the US, moving to serve its 30M+ American customers directly instead of through partner banks. Owning the licence would let the BNPL company issue credit and debit products itself, capturing margin it currently shares and building on the $93.1B in credit it has already routed to US consumers. Revolut and Bunq both filed for US licences earlier this year, and the Trump administration's openness to new entrants is turning a market that long resisted European fintechs into the next battleground.

title: Anthropic inks $19B AI data center lease with TeraWulf
blurb: Anthropic agreed to pay $19B over 20 years to lease a TeraWulf data center being built on a former Kentucky aluminum smelter, with 401 megawatts coming fully online in 2028. The site's existing power and fiber infrastructure lets construction skip the years-long wait for new transmission lines, and TeraWulf plans to spend $3B to $4B building it, less than a fifth of the lease's value against last quarter's $34M in revenue. The scale of these commitments shows frontier labs locking up power and land years ahead of demand, turning compute capacity into the constraint that defines who can compete.

title: Microsoft lays off nearly 5,000 employees across Xbox, commercial sales
blurb: Microsoft cut around 4,800 roles, or 2.1% of its workforce, with Xbox absorbing 1,600 of them and about 3,200 total gaming cuts coming through fiscal 2027. Leadership insists the eliminated roles are not being replaced by AI, but the layoffs land alongside a $2.5B bet on its new Frontier Company unit for enterprise AI deployments. The pattern founders should watch is job cuts correlating with rising AI spend, as even a company posting Xbox margins 3-10x below its platform peers reallocates payroll toward compute and forward-deployed engineers.

title: Survey: Chinese companies plan to allocate 46% of their AI accelerator budget to domestic products in the next 12 months, up from 30% today, a shift from Nvidia (Gao Yuan/Bloomberg)
blurb: Chinese companies plan to route 46% of their AI accelerator budget to domestic chips over the next 12 months, up from 30% today, according to a new survey. The shift pulls demand away from Nvidia, whose China business already runs into export controls, and toward local suppliers like Huawei and Cambricon. For founders building on Chinese infrastructure or selling into the market, the compute stack is splitting in two, and betting on a single global hardware supply chain is getting riskier by the quarter.

title: US autonomous military vehicle startup Forterra says it deployed 100+ Lancer UGVs, based on Polaris ATVs, in Ukraine since 2025, completing 1,100+ missions (Tim Fernholz/TechCrunch)
blurb: Forterra says it has deployed more than 100 Lancer unmanned ground vehicles, built on Polaris ATVs, in Ukraine since 2025, running over 1,100 missions. The battlefield is turning into the fastest proving ground for autonomy, where cheap, rugged, expendable platforms log real mileage that no test track can match. Defense-tech startups now have something most AI companies still lack: a live customer running their systems at scale, under fire, and paying for hardware that gets destroyed and reordered.

title: Google and RWE Back German Nuclear Startup Proxima Fusion at €2.4 Billion Valuation
blurb: Fusion funding keeps flowing to hardware that won't produce power for a decade. Proxima Fusion, a German stellarator startup, raised €411M ($469M) at a €2.4B valuation, with Google and national energy firm RWE among the backers. The plant it's building isn't slated to run until the 2030s, yet strategic money, an energy incumbent and a hyperscaler chasing clean baseload for AI compute, is committing now rather than waiting for a working reactor.

title: Chinese Firms Leave Nvidia for Local AI Suppliers, Survey Shows
blurb: Chinese firms are dropping Nvidia's advanced accelerators for domestic silicon, according to a new survey, as US export tensions push Beijing's tech-substitution drive into the AI infrastructure buildout. The shift signals that the world's second-largest AI market is decoupling its hardware stack from American suppliers rather than waiting out the restrictions. For any startup counting on China as a customer or a supply chain, the AI stack is fracturing into separate US and Chinese ecosystems that increasingly run on different chips.

title: Chinese AI models are gaining ground with U.S. companies as OpenAI, Anthropic costs surge
blurb: Chinese open-weight models are pulling U.S. companies away from OpenAI and Anthropic as the performance gap narrows and token prices at the top labs keep climbing. On OpenRouter, U.S. traffic to Chinese models has stayed above 30% every week since February and hit 46%, up from an 11% average over the prior year. The switch is now a cost decision rather than a capability one, and companies that adopted frontier models at any price are getting cost-conscious. Lindy moved 100% of its traffic from Claude to DeepSeek in June.

title: By Year-End, Anthropic Will Out-Earn Every Public Software Company Except Microsoft
blurb: Anthropic exited 2025 at a $9B run-rate and hit $47B by mid-May 2026, disclosed in its Series H, which puts it past Salesforce, Adobe, Intuit, ServiceNow, and Workday three years after its first dollar of revenue. On its current trajectory it tracks toward $70B to $90B by December, leaving only Microsoft's roughly $300B software and cloud business ahead of it. Oracle and IBM look like exceptions, but strip out the GPU-rental and consulting revenue and their actual software lines sit below Anthropic too.

title: Google backs Proxima Fusion in €411m raise
blurb: Proxima Fusion raised €411m at a €2.5bn valuation, with Google joining its cap table in the internet giant's first backing of a European fusion company. The Munich startup is building Alpha, a €2bn demonstration stellarator that suspends and superheats hydrogen plasma to fuse its nuclei, with completion slated for the early 2030s and a first commercial plant in the mid-2030s. The round plus €400m from Bavaria brings Alpha's funding to €800m, and Proxima is now chasing the remaining €1.2bn from a German federal grant expected to be tendered later this year.

title: Plaid talks with banks about potential IPO
blurb: Plaid is in early talks with banks about a potential IPO, though the company says no decision is final and a listing is not imminent. It last raised $575M in April 2025 at a $6.1B valuation, which CEO Zach Perret called its final private round before going public. The timing matters because Plaid is broadening beyond bank-linking into credit risk, with a new transaction-sequence model that cut default risk by 13.6% at a 70% approval rate, positioning it as more than an infrastructure layer heading into public markets.

title: Microsoft cuts 4,800 jobs, as Xbox unit downsizes and plans to spin off four gaming studios
blurb: Microsoft eliminated 4,800 jobs, about 2.1% of its workforce, and is gutting Xbox by roughly 20% while spinning four gaming studios back out as independents. The cuts land as Microsoft trails every megacap peer in 2026, down 19% on investor fears that generative AI will hollow out the enterprise software it sells, even as its own AI products have yet to land. The signal for operators is that owning the platform no longer protects the businesses built on top of it, and even a $3T incumbent is shedding the units where AI hasn't found traction.

title: Paris-based UMA, founded by ex-Tesla Optimus scientist Rémi Cadene and ex-Google DeepMind researcher Pierre Sermanet, demos its Northstar AI humanoid robot (Benoit Berthelot/Bloomberg)
blurb: UMA, a Paris startup founded by ex-Tesla Optimus scientist Rémi Cadene and ex-Google DeepMind researcher Pierre Sermanet, demoed its Northstar humanoid robot. The founder pedigree matters more than the demo, because humanoid robotics is a talent-constrained field and Europe has struggled to keep researchers from decamping to US labs. UMA is a signal that the frontier robotics race now has a credible European entrant, backed by people who built the systems everyone else is trying to copy.

title: AI Legal Startup Norm Valued at $1.2 Billion in Funding Round
blurb: Norm Ai raised $120M at a $1.2B valuation to automate legal work and rethink how firms bill for it. The bet targets the billable hour itself, the pricing model that has defined legal economics for decades, by shifting the work AI can absorb toward outcomes instead of time. For founders, the signal is that AI-native entrants are now attacking not just the labor in professional services but the revenue model underneath it, and the incumbents most exposed are the ones whose margins depend on billing for hours.

title: Munich-based nuclear fusion startup Proxima Fusion raised €411M led by trading firm XTX and UK-based East X at a €2.4B valuation, with participation from Google (Bloomberg)
blurb: Proxima Fusion, a Munich-based nuclear fusion startup, raised €411M at a €2.4B valuation, led by trading firms XTX and East X with Google participating. The round is one of Europe's largest fusion bets and notable for who wrote the checks: quant trading houses, not the usual deep-tech VCs, are now underwriting decade-long hardware risk. Fusion has become a place where firms with long time horizons and deep balance sheets can deploy capital that traditional venture funds, pressed for returns, increasingly can't.

title: Geothermal Startup Raises $144 Million to Drill Superhot Rocks
blurb: Quaise Energy raised $144 million to develop drilling technology that reaches superhot rock, where temperatures unlock geothermal energy nearly anywhere rather than just volcanic zones. The bet is that repurposing oil-and-gas drilling techniques can tap heat several miles down and turn geothermal from a regional niche into baseload power that competes with fossil fuels. The capital signals that investors are still funding hard-tech energy plays with long timelines, betting the drilling problem is an engineering one rather than a scientific one.

title: SAP’s Senior Reshuffle Follows AI-Induced Stock Rout
blurb: SAP is reshuffling its senior ranks after its stock fell 46% under CEO Christian Klein, as customers delay big ERP renewals and route budgets toward AI instead. The company that spent decades locking enterprises into multi-year suites is now watching those same contracts become the thing slowing it down. The lesson for founders selling into the enterprise is that the incumbent's installed base is only a moat while the buyer still believes the roadmap, and AI has made a lot of buyers stop believing.

title: Nvidia-Backed Startup Nscale Locks in $900 Million for Data-Center Buildout
blurb: Nscale, the UK AI-infrastructure startup backed by Nvidia, raised $900M to fund data-center buildouts across Europe, the US, and Asia Pacific. The round adds flexible liquidity to accelerate capacity as demand for AI compute keeps outrunning supply. The signal for founders is that the money and attention are moving down the stack toward the picks-and-shovels layer, where whoever controls compute capacity sets the terms for everyone building on top.

title: Microsoft Lays Off Thousands of Xbox Employees, Closes Game Studios
blurb: Microsoft laid off thousands of Xbox employees and shut down game studios as part of wider cuts, redirecting spending toward AI. The gaming division posted record revenue last year, so this isn't a struggling business getting trimmed. It's capital being pulled from a profitable unit to feed compute capex, and the same logic is playing out across big tech, where AI buildout is now funded by cutting everything that isn't AI.

title: Nvidia taps AI cloud providers to expand compute access for startups
blurb: Nvidia launched a partnership program that routes fast-growing AI startups to its network of cloud providers in exchange for a cut of both product and cloud revenue. The initial capacity comes from two Australian firms, with Sharon AI deploying up to 40,000 GPUs and Firmus building a 360-megawatt data center in Indonesia for as many as 170,000 GPUs. The move turns scarce compute into a financing lever, and it follows a wave of revenue- and equity-sharing deals between AI companies and chipmakers as the sector works around a liquidity crunch.

title: Microsoft betting that enterprise AI needs engineers, not bigger sales teams
blurb: Microsoft cut around 4,800 employees this week, roughly 2.1% of its workforce, mostly in commercial sales and Xbox, days after launching Frontier Company to embed its own engineers alongside customers deploying AI. The bet is that AI adoption gets won by implementation expertise, not account management, so the traditional sales-and-support layer is what gets trimmed. For founders selling into the enterprise, the signal is that buyers increasingly want engineers who can ship the deployment, not reps who can pitch it, and the vendors reorganizing around that are the ones setting the pace.

title: Price per 1M tokens is meaningless
blurb: Comparing models by price per 1M tokens is the fastest way to misread your AI bill, because each lab runs its own tokenizer and the same text splits into wildly different token counts. Anthropic recently changed its tokenizer and Claude started splitting identical text into 30% more tokens, and most of your spend goes to hidden "thinking" tokens whose length varies model to model. On the Artificial Analysis benchmark, GPT-5.5 is nominally pricier than Claude Opus 4.8 yet finishes tasks at nearly half the cost, so the only number that matters is cost per completed task.

title: US investors will soon get access to SK Hynix, another memory maker riding the AI boom
blurb: SK Hynix is planning a US IPO of nearly 17.8 million shares in ADR form, a raise that could hit around $28 billion and hand American investors direct exposure to the AI memory boom. First-quarter revenue jumped nearly 200% year over year and the stock is up about 260% this year, as hyperscalers building AI data centers push demand past supply into a shortage dubbed "RAMageddon." The bet is that Wall Street wants another Nvidia, but the $550 billion South Korean chipmakers are spending on new capacity could glut the market and crash prices by the time it comes online.

title: Vercel CEO Guillermo Rauch on the fight to split off models from agents
blurb: Vercel has quietly become one of AI software's most central companies, running 6 million deployments a day with half triggered by coding agents and more than 1 trillion tokens flowing through its AI gateway daily. CEO Guillermo Rauch argues the two killer apps are the coding agent and the internal agent that runs the company, and both create the same problem: securing data access, auditing tool calls, and controlling what leaves the sandbox. His bet is that the value moves to the platform layer that governs agents, not the labs building the models underneath.

title: UK-based AI infrastructure startup Nscale secures a $900M line of credit to expand its data center buildout across Europe, the US, and the Asia Pacific (Mauro Orru/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: Nscale, a UK-based AI infrastructure startup, secured a $900M line of credit to expand its data center buildout across Europe, the US, and Asia Pacific. Debt, not equity, is increasingly how the compute buildout gets funded, because lenders will underwrite physical assets and signed capacity contracts that venture dollars won't stretch to cover. The race to stand up AI capacity is now as much a financing problem as an engineering one, and the startups winning it are the ones who can raise against their hardware.

title: OpenAI, Anthropic, and other top AI labs are offering startups token credits and special promotions, as the AI labs seek lasting streams of enterprise revenue (Wall Street Journal)
blurb: OpenAI, Anthropic, and other frontier labs are handing startups token credits and special promotions to lock in enterprise spend early. The play is to win the workloads while switching costs are low, then convert free and discounted usage into durable enterprise revenue as those startups scale. For founders, the credits are real leverage now, but building your core product on subsidized tokens means your unit economics are set by a supplier who is also racing to move up the stack into your market.

title: Google and RWE back Proxima Fusion in record €411mn round
blurb: Munich-based Proxima Fusion raised €411mn ($468mn) at a €2.4bn valuation, the largest private fusion round Europe has seen, with Google and utility RWE joining as strategic backers. The round was led by the venture arms of two quant trading firms and clears more than €650mn raised in under three years, for a technology that has yet to produce a watt of commercial power. The cash funds Alpha, a net-energy demonstrator Proxima wants running near Munich in the early 2030s, before a first commercial stellarator plant later in the decade.

title: Samsung’s Q2 profit jumps 19-fold to 89 trillion won on AI memory demand
blurb: Samsung guided to about 89.4 trillion won ($58.4bn) in Q2 operating profit, roughly 19 times a year earlier and, by most counts, the largest quarterly operating profit any tech company has ever posted. The driver is pricing, not volume: contract DRAM prices rose about 44% quarter on quarter and NAND about 53% as AI server demand outran what the three big memory makers can supply. What sets this cycle apart is breadth, with the surge now spilling out of high-bandwidth memory into ordinary DRAM and NAND as inference build-outs scale.

title: Clouded Judgement 7.3.26 - The End of Compute Scarcity? Not So Fast
blurb: SpaceX renting out about 450k GPUs for roughly $2.32B a month and Meta planning a cloud business look like signs the compute crunch is ending, but the author argues the bear case falls apart on the details. The SpaceX deals carry 90-day outs on both sides and command extraordinarily high prices, which only happens when buyers like Anthropic and Google cannot get capacity anywhere else. Sellers structured them that way because they intend to use the compute themselves later, not because demand is drying up.

title: Amazon Returns to US Bond Market to Fund AI Infrastructure Build
blurb: Amazon is returning to the US bond market to help fund its AI infrastructure buildout, tapping debt to cover a data-center and compute expansion that operating cash flow no longer fully absorbs. The move puts one of the most cash-rich companies in tech alongside Meta, Oracle, and Alphabet in borrowing against the AI capex cycle. When hyperscalers with hundreds of billions in reserves choose to issue bonds rather than self-fund, the capital intensity of the buildout has reached a scale that reprices what infrastructure competition costs everyone downstream.

title: VC Money Doubles In Defense Tech as the Next Big Bet
blurb: Venture money flowing into defense tech doubled over the past year, as aerospace and military startups shed the taboo that once kept Silicon Valley out. The category that most firms wouldn't touch a decade ago is now where they're placing their biggest bets, betting that government contracts and geopolitical tension will underwrite durable demand. The shift matters because the capital, talent, and founder attention chasing consumer and SaaS ideas is now competing with a sector where the moats are regulatory, the sales cycles are long, and the incumbents are primes rather than startups.

title: SpaceX Joins Nasdaq 100 as Wall Street Makes Bullish Calls
blurb: SpaceX joins the Nasdaq 100 on Tuesday, and Wall Street brokerages are opening coverage of Musk's rocket, satellite, and AI company with a near-unanimous buy call. The listing turns one of the most valuable private companies of the past decade into a public benchmark that index funds now have to hold. For founders, it marks the moment SpaceX stops being a venture story and becomes a market one, with its Starlink and launch economics priced daily against the rest of the index.

title: Savi’s app aims to protect consumers from realistic AI scams like kidnappers demanding ransom
blurb: Savi Security raised $7M in seed funding, led by Acrew Capital, to shield ordinary consumers from AI-generated scams routed through texts, emails, and phone calls. The founders, both veterans of enterprise cybersecurity, started the company after a scammer spoofed their sister's number and voice to stage a fake kidnapping and demand ransom from their mother. The shift underneath is economic: attacks that once required deep research and custom voice-spoofing tech were only worth aiming at Fortune 500 targets, but cheap, powerful LLMs now make that same sophistication profitable to point at everyday people.

title: Wall Street Buys Into Musk’s SpaceX Vision
blurb: SpaceX is joining the Nasdaq 100, and analysts at the big global brokers are now telling investors to buy the shares. It marks the moment Wall Street stops treating Musk's space bet as a private-market curiosity and starts pricing it like a blue chip. For founders, the signal is that public markets will underwrite deeply capital-intensive, long-horizon hardware plays again, not just asset-light software, when the growth story is credible enough.

title: Apple veteran's Chinese smart-glasses firm becomes unicorn as Tencent, Meituan fund rival to Meta
blurb: Even Realities, a Shenzhen smart-glasses maker founded by an Apple Watch and iPhone veteran, raised $150M in a pre-Series B at a $1B valuation, with Tencent and Meituan backing a direct challenge to Meta. Its bet is the opposite of Meta's camera-equipped Ray-Bans: no camera, no recording, just notifications, navigation, and live translation on a heads-up display, sold on privacy. The category shipped 2.25 million units last quarter, up 167% year over year, but Meta still holds nearly 70% share, so the opening is real and the incumbent lead is steep.

title: Nscale closes a $900m revolving credit facility to fund its data-centre build-out
blurb: Nscale closed a $900m revolving credit facility syndicated across a dozen banks, including J.P. Morgan, Goldman Sachs, and Morgan Stanley, to accelerate its data-centre build-out across the US, Europe, and Asia-Pacific. The signal is the syndicate, not the size: that roster is the kind usually assembled for an established borrower, not a company founded two years ago that raised a $2bn Series C at a $14.6bn valuation in March. A revolver bridges the gap AI infrastructure builders all face, committing to chips, power, and construction long before rental revenue arrives, and it adds firepower without diluting shareholders across a fourth round.

title: BAE Systems and Keysight back Expeditions’ €197M fund for European defence tech
blurb: Expeditions closed its second fund at €197M, more than twice its original target, and pulled in BAE Systems and Keysight Technologies as limited partners rather than just customers of its portfolio companies. The Warsaw and London firm also drew the European Investment Fund, Polish Development Fund, and NATO Innovation Fund, with Cambridge Associates ranking Fund I in the top 0.01% of funds worldwide. When prime contractors start writing LP checks into defence venture, the line between strategic customer and capital source is collapsing, and European defence tech is where that money is landing.

title: Nscale banks $900M from J.P. Morgan and Goldman Sachs: Is AI infrastructure debt the next bubble?
blurb: Nscale, a London AI cloud platform, closed a $900M revolving credit facility from J.P. Morgan, Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley, and a dozen other banks, its third debt deal in five months after a $1.4B GPU-backed loan and a $790M raise for its Norway campus. That pushes total capital past $6.3B against a $14.6B valuation, most of it borrowed rather than sold. The pattern to watch is that AI infrastructure is now funded like real estate, with banks lending against data centres and power contracts, which loads the buildout with debt that only pays off if compute demand keeps outrunning supply.

title: Smart glasses maker Even Realities hits $1B valuation with $150M funding led by Meituan, Tencent
blurb: Even Realities, a three-year-old Shenzhen startup founded by ex-Apple engineers, raised $150M at a $1B valuation in a pre-Series B round led by Meituan and Tencent. While Meta and Snap chase camera-equipped glasses built around capture and content, Even is betting the opposite way: display-first frames that beam information into the wearer's line of sight, no camera, with privacy designed into the hardware and software. The wedge is working. Its camera-free G2 helped Even become the first in the category to sell more than 10,000 pairs, and headcount jumped from 30-40 in 2024 to 300-400 today.

title: Why Skello’s founders own more of the company after a €200M raise
blurb: Skello raised €200 million from Bridgepoint while its founders and management increased their own stake, an inversion of how rounds this size usually work. The French HR platform for shift workers crossed €50 million in annual recurring revenue in 2026 and is already profitable, covering 30,000 businesses and 700,000 daily users. That profitability is what let the founders negotiate up rather than down, raising capital to fund European acquisitions and roughly 100 new hires rather than to survive.

title: Alibaba's Qwen models have made it an AI powerhouse, but the company has struggled to turn their global popularity into a profitable business (New York Times)
blurb: Alibaba's Qwen models have turned it into one of the world's most-used open-source AI labs, but that popularity hasn't translated into profit. Free and widely downloaded models drive adoption and developer mindshare without a clear path to revenue, leaving Alibaba to fund the compute while others build the businesses on top. The gap between global influence and a working monetization model is the same trap facing every lab betting that reach today converts to margin later.

title: Tech Weakness Resumes After Samsung Misses Lofty AI Expectations
blurb: Samsung's earnings missed elevated AI expectations, sending US tech futures lower and renewing pressure on chipmakers across global markets. The miss matters because Samsung sits upstream of the entire AI hardware buildout, so a soft print there reads as a signal about memory and component demand, not just one company's quarter. For founders, it's a reminder that the market has priced AI infrastructure for perfection, and any earnings that fall short of that bar now moves the whole sector.

title: Court filing: Meta says four US states seek $1.4T over claims it designed Facebook and Instagram to addict youth and misled the public; its market cap is ~$1.5T (Diana Novak Jones/Reuters)
blurb: Four US states are seeking $1.4T from Meta, alleging it deliberately engineered Facebook and Instagram to addict young users and hid the risks from the public. The demand nearly equals Meta's entire market cap of around $1.5T, making it an existential number rather than a line-item settlement. For founders building consumer products, it signals that "engagement optimization" is becoming a legal liability, and that regulators are now willing to price alleged harm at the scale of the whole company.

title: AI Giants Are Handing Out Tons of Free Computing Power to Grab Startup Share
blurb: AI labs are giving away large amounts of free compute to lock in startups before rivals can. OpenAI, Anthropic, and the cloud giants are subsidizing early usage to win business users and build the recurring revenue streams they still lack. The bet is that credits create switching costs, so the startup that builds on your free tokens today becomes a paying enterprise account tomorrow. For founders, the free compute is real leverage now, but the pricing you lock in matters more once the subsidies run out.

title: BAE-Backed Venture Capital Firm Expeditions Raises €197 Million Fund for European Defense Startups
blurb: European defense tech now has its own dedicated capital pool, as Expeditions closed a €197 million ($225 million) fund backed by BAE Systems and the NATO Innovation Fund. The mandate is explicitly sovereign, funding startups that build military technology Europe can own and control rather than import. Strategic investors anchoring a defense fund signals that the continent's rearmament is becoming an investable category, not just a policy talking point, and founders in dual-use hardware now have a buyer that understands the procurement path.

title: Smart glasses startup Even Realities raises $150M
blurb: Even Realities raised $150M at a $1B valuation, led by Meituan with Tencent participating, to scale its $599 Even G2 smart glasses. The device skips a camera and instead pairs a dual-layer MicroLED display with an AI assistant and an optional health-tracking ring. The round is the latest in a run of eyewear bets this year, after Xreal's $100M in January and IXI's $35M, as startups position against Meta in the consumer smart glasses market.

title: Lisbon-based BIZAY raises €48.7 million Series D; set to surpass €88.6 million revenue and reach profitability
blurb: BIZAY, the Lisbon-based platform behind what it calls the world's largest catalogue of customised products, raised €48.75M in a Series D led by existing investor Indico Capital. The company expects to cross €87.4M in annual revenue for the first time and post its first profitable year, built on production software it says cuts costs by 80%. The capital funds a US push into the largest market for customised products and a roll-up of a fragmented sector estimated near €787M, run from Portugal.

title: London hyperscaler Nscale secures £670m credit facility
blurb: Nscale secured a $900m revolving credit facility from a syndicate led by J.P. Morgan, Goldman Sachs, and Morgan Stanley, its latest raise after a record $1.1bn Series B, a multi-billion Series C, and a half-billion-pound round earlier this year. The revolving structure lets the London hyperscaler draw, repay, and redraw capital to fund data center construction across the US, Europe, and APAC. Institutional debt from the biggest banks, on top of the venture rounds, signals that appetite for physical AI infrastructure now extends well past equity investors into the balance sheets that fund real assets.

title: Tech Volatility Hits Highest Since Dot-Com Bust Next to S&P 500
blurb: Tech stock volatility relative to the broader S&P 500 has climbed to its highest level since the dot-com bust, as the gap between high-flying tech names and the rest of the market widens. The bigger the rally in a handful of AI and tech leaders, the more fragile the group looks when sentiment turns. For founders raising or planning an exit, this signals a market that could reprice tech risk quickly, so timing and runway assumptions built on today's multiples carry more downside than they did six months ago.

title: Tencent Sells $1.5 Billion Kuaishou Stake as AI Pivot Quickens
blurb: Tencent sold $1.5B of Kuaishou stock, trimming a mature internet holding to fund its shift toward AI. The move fits a broader pattern of Chinese tech giants recycling capital out of legacy consumer bets and into compute, models, and infrastructure. When a company that once bought stakes to lock in ecosystem reach starts selling them, it signals that the returns on the next decade look different from the last one.

title: Samsung’s Record Profit Fails to Impress After AI Chip Rally
blurb: Samsung's quarterly profit surged 19-fold on runaway demand for AI memory, yet the stock sold off as investors cashed out of a near-150% rally this year. The move shows how far AI expectations have already been priced into the chip names, so even a blowout beat clears a bar the market set months ago. For founders, it is a reminder that in an AI-hyped market you are judged against the story already baked into your valuation, not against last quarter's numbers.

title: Mag 7 Loses Market Swagger as AI Trade Spreads Beyond Behemoths
blurb: The Magnificent Seven no longer sets the market's direction, as the AI trade broadens to the companies actually deploying AI rather than the handful selling the picks and shovels. For years the seven giants dominated the S&P 500 and dictated where the index went, but that concentration is loosening as gains spread across the rest of the market. For founders, it signals that the AI premium is moving down the stack, rewarding the operators who turn the technology into revenue rather than the labs and chipmakers supplying it.

title: Agility Robotics is going public at $2.5B while Figure AI sits at $39B on less proven ground — and the gap between those two numbers is the entire humanoid robotics thesis in one line
blurb: Agility Robotics is going public via SPAC at roughly $2.5B, a fraction of the $39B Figure AI carries and the $5.5B-plus of Apptronik, despite ten years of deployment history and booked multi-year revenue under a robots-as-a-service model. The valuation gap is the humanoid thesis in one line: private markets are paying for demos while Agility is priced on paying logistics and manufacturing customers. Being among the first humanoids on public markets creates a scarcity trade, but it also puts that inflated-multiple question somewhere it can finally be tested.

title: June 2026's top 10 European tech deals you need to know about
blurb: European tech recorded 293 funding deals worth €8.3B in June, a 14% jump in deal count but a 21% drop in capital from May's €10.5B. Germany replaced the UK as the top hub with €2.4B, and robotics overtook cloud as the leading sector after pulling in €1.3B, led by Neura Robotics' $1.4B round. The money is concentrating where Europe is chasing industrial resilience and digital sovereignty, with the month's biggest checks going to robotics, AI, security, space, and quantum while the top 10 deals accounted for 59% of all funding.

title: Station F ramps up as a launchpad for Europe’s hottest AI startups
blurb: Station F is turning its size and Xavier Niel's connections into a distribution machine for European AI startups, and its F/ai accelerator is the sharp end. The first cohort was backed by nearly every frontier lab and cloud provider (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, Meta, Mistral, AWS, Microsoft), with Eleven Labs, GitHub, HubSpot, and Rippling joining the second batch in September. The program targets €1M in revenue within six months, which signals where European accelerators are headed: less demo-day theater, more proof that the company can actually sell.

title: Nvidia Supplier Unimicron Raises $1.4 Billion in GDS Sale
blurb: Unimicron raised $1.36B selling global depositary shares, riding a stock that has climbed more than 700% in the past 12 months on AI demand. The Taiwanese firm makes the advanced substrates that go under Nvidia's chips, and that once-unglamorous supplier layer is now flush enough to fund its own capacity expansion. When the picks-and-shovels vendors two tiers down from the AI labs can raise over a billion on public markets, the capital is reaching far past the model builders.

title: Will Someone Finally Blink in the AI Spending War?
blurb: The AI spending war has reached the point where the biggest buildout in tech history could tip into oversupply. If Meta starts renting out excess compute, it would be the clearest signal yet that hyperscalers have overbuilt, turning a strategic asset into a commodity they need to offload. The question underneath the capex race is who blinks first, because the company that admits it has too much capacity resets the terms for everyone still racing to add more.

title: Sector Snapshot: Cleantech Startup Funding Stabilizes As Energy Demand Grows
blurb: Cleantech funding has stabilized rather than recovered, with investors putting $15B into seed-through-growth rounds in the first half of 2026, on track to slightly beat 2025's multi-year low. Q2 alone drew about $8B, the highest quarterly total since 2024, but the sector still sits far below its 2021-2022 peak and now commands a smaller share of total venture as AI absorbs the capital. The money is concentrating in heavy bets: green steel producer Stegra's $1.6B round led the year, followed by Slate Auto's $650M and fusion deals from Helion and Inertia.

title: Your Churn Threshold Is a Pricing Decision
blurb: Default classification thresholds are secretly a pricing decision, and on the most-recycled churn dataset on Kaggle they are wrong by a factor of 13. Across 36 public IBM Telco analyses, nearly all report accuracy or F1, only one in seven plots a profit curve, and none price a misclassification in dollars. That gap leaves about $86 per customer on the floor, or $8.6M across a 100,000-subscriber book. What stays invariant is the asymmetry: missing a churner costs 13 times more than over-treating a loyalist, so sweep the threshold against real costs instead of defaulting to 0.5.

title: ECMAScript 2026 specification approved
blurb: ECMA International approved ECMAScript 2026, the 17th edition of the JavaScript spec, on June 30. New additions include Math.sumPrecise for low-error summation, Array.fromAsync for building arrays from async iterables, Error.isError for reliable error detection, Uint8Array hex and base64 conversion methods, and Map getOrInsert helpers for default values on missing keys.

title: Hunting a 16-year-old SQLite bug with TLA+: is dqlite affected?
blurb: The dqlite team at Canonical used TLA+ to model the 16-year-old SQLite WAL checkpoint bug that can corrupt a database, building a formal spec of the lock and shared-memory sequence that triggers corruption. They then modeled how dqlite drives SQLite and checked the spec to determine whether dqlite's usage pattern can reach the same faulty state.

title: Your Worker can now have its own cache in front of it
blurb: Cloudflare launched Workers Cache, a tiered cache that sits in front of a Worker, enabled with a single `cache: { enabled: true }` block in Wrangler and driven by the standard Cache-Control and Cache-Tag headers you already use. On a cache hit the Worker never runs, so you skip the CPU billing, and the cache follows the Worker across custom domains, workers.dev, service bindings, and previews, with per-entrypoint control and programmatic purge by tag or path prefix.

title: Ternlight – 7 MB embedding model that runs in browser (WASM)
blurb: Ternlight is a text embedding model that ships as a single npm package and runs entirely in the browser on CPU via WASM, with engine plus weights totaling 7 MB and a 5 MB mini variant. It embeds text in roughly 5 ms with no model download step, no GPU, and no server calls, letting you run semantic search and similarity ranking fully client-side.

title: Apple Container: Open-source tool for Linux containers on the Mac
blurb: Apple released Container 1.0.0, an open-source Swift tool that runs each Linux workload as its own lightweight VM on Apple silicon, using OCI-compatible images so developers can pull from and push to any standard registry. The release adds a container machine command for persistent Linux environments, a container cp command for host-to-container file transfers, and moves configuration to a config.toml file, a breaking change that requires macOS 26.

title: Python 3.14 compiled to metal – no interpreter
blurb: pon is a JIT and AoT native compiler and runtime for Python 3.14, written in Rust with no interpreter or bytecode: it parses modules with the ruff parser, lowers them to one shared IR, and compiles to machine code through Cranelift, either in-process or into a standalone executable. It swaps reference counting for a Green Tea garbage collector and enforces correctness with a byte-exact differential harness against CPython 3.14.0.

title: Prompting Claude Fable 5
blurb: Anthropic's guide documents the prompting and scaffolding changes needed to move from Claude Opus 4.8 to Claude Fable 5, which targets long-running, ambiguous, end-to-end tasks that take a person hours to weeks. It covers behavioral differences that require prompt tuning, adaptive-thinking API changes, and configuring automatic fallback to Opus 4.8 when safety classifiers return a refusal stop reason.

title: North Korean Hackers Target Open Source Developers in Supply Chain Attacks
blurb: Socket disclosed PolinRider, a North Korean supply chain campaign running since December 2025 that compromises maintainer accounts across NPM, Packagist, Go modules, and Chrome extensions to push the DEV#POPPER RAT and OmniStealer via obfuscated JavaScript loaders. Socket has found 162 malicious release artifacts across 108 packages, with attackers rewriting Git history to age the changes and pulling encrypted payloads from blockchain RPC infrastructure.

title: Is Three the Magic Number? An Empirical Evaluation of LLM-Based Repair Loops
blurb: This paper evaluates how repair-loop iteration limits affect LLM-based software engineering workflows across code generation, test generation, and code translation. The authors find consistent diminishing returns, with the first three to four iterations capturing most gains, and show that workflow orchestration and feedback design matter more than the underlying model.

title: Pruning RAG context down to what the answer actually needs
blurb: Kapa added a third step to their RAG pipeline: a small, cheap LLM that reads the question and all retrieved chunks together and drops the ones the answer won't need before the expensive generator sees them. The pruner cuts about 68% of context while keeping roughly 96% of recall, lowering per-query cost by a third net of its own overhead, since retrieved chunks account for two-thirds of a query's spend.

title: Let Fable use its own judgement rather than dictating how it should work
blurb: The author argues that instructing coding agents to use their own judgement produces better results than dictating explicit rules, citing advice from the Claude Code team to let the model decide when tests are warranted rather than encoding rigid thresholds. Extending the idea to cost, he prompts the agent to delegate implementation to a lower-power model in a subagent while keeping design and review on the main loop, which stretched his token allowance further.

title: Full Writeup of the Windows GDID
blurb: A reverse-engineering writeup traces the Windows "Global Device Identifier" that surfaced in the July 2026 Scattered Spider criminal complaint, showing it is a 64-bit Microsoft Account Device PUID provisioned by wlidsvc, cached in the registry, and reported through Connected Devices Platform and Delivery Optimization. The author debunks the viral claim that it is hardware-derived, notes a reinstall mints a new ID, and tags every finding as court-sourced, live-observed, or static-analysis proven.

title: Parallel $\mathcal O(\sqrt n)$ Overhead LSD Radix Sort
blurb: Radsort is a stable LSD radix sort variant that uses only O(√n) additional space instead of a full out-of-place buffer, while staying simple to implement and easy to parallelize. It beats conventional out-of-place LSD radix sort on arrays larger than roughly 2 MiB, trading a small asymptotic space overhead for lower memory pressure at scale.

title: thundersnap 0.01: an undo button for everything
blurb: thundersnap is an open source tool from apenwarr that makes replicating, forking, sharing, and running container snapshots fast across cloud and personal hardware, using Tailscale to stitch bare-metal machines and rented boxes into a single mesh. The 0.01 release pitches itself as an undo button for both humans and AI agents, trading ephemeral containers for persistent disk, portable snapshots, and the freedom to move workloads to whichever provider offers the cheapest hardware.

title: Fable turned reMarkable into Tom Riddle's diary from Harry Potter
blurb: Fable turns a reMarkable Paper Pro into an ink-only LLM interface: write with the pen, and a handwritten reply appears on the page.

title: Rebuilding Cognition's Agentic MapReduce
blurb: The author reverse-engineered Cognition's security-focused Agentic MapReduce into a general-purpose loop for research, coding, and review, using git branches and merges as the primary agent-to-agent communication layer. Subagents shard across the codebase by folder and commit to their own branches, a reducer agent dedupes and merges compatible findings into a candidate branch, and each mapping iteration decides whether to continue from a candidate, a prior worker branch, or a fresh shard.

title: Beyond Compliance: A Large Scale Study on the Completeness and Consistency of the GitHub SBOMs
blurb: A large-scale study analyzed GitHub's auto-generated SBOMs across 10,000 repositories in ten language ecosystems, comparing them against Syft, Trivy, and the Microsoft SBOM Tool. GitHub SBOMs fail NTIA compliance but keep core metadata intact, match the Microsoft tool, and often beat Syft and Trivy on version and license data, with completeness varying sharply by how each ecosystem manages dependencies.

title: Kani: A Model Checker for Rust
blurb: Kani is an open-source bounded model checker for Rust that compiles proof harnesses from MIR into CBMC's verification engine, checking safety properties like unsafe soundness, functional correctness, and panic-freedom with no annotations required. Function and loop contracts extend it from bounded to unbounded verification, and it runs in production CI at over 16,000 harnesses per code change in the Rust standard library campaign, where it uncovered six previously unknown bugs.

title: Migrating Counter Service storage: Design choices and learnings
blurb: Grab migrated its Counter Service, which answers time-windowed count queries for fraud detection at tens of thousands of QPS and roughly a billion requests per day, from a wide-column store to Aerospike while decoupling storage from business logic. The post details the reader-side safety layer, a rewritten writer-side data model that collapsed the original four network round-trips per event, and what broke during the gradual rollout.

title: Road to Elm 1.0
blurb: Elm 0.19.2 cuts parser allocations to speed up compilation, hitting 20% lower GC copying, 10% lower peak memory, and 7% faster builds overall, with one 351-module project dropping from 4.981s to 2.595s from scratch. Evan Czaplicki frames the release as the first step toward Elm 1.0, a planned series of small non-breaking releases that will fold in compiler and language ideas like equatable and hashable types from his Acadia work.

title: Meet Kirki: WordPress’s First Visual Builder With An Infinite Canvas
blurb: Kirki is a no-code WordPress visual builder built around a freeform infinite canvas, with an integrated CMS and built-in features that aim to remove the third-party plugin dependencies and bloated output of traditional page builders. A free version is available on WordPress.org, and this sponsored review compares Kirki against conventional builders on pricing, site performance, ease of use, and theme compatibility.

title: Dissecting Apple's Sparse Image Format (ASIF)
blurb: The author reverse-engineers ASIF, the sparse disk image format Apple introduced in macOS Tahoe for its Virtualization framework, walking through the process of writing a parser from scratch. The post starts with eyeballing hexdumps and a numbered test pattern, then decodes the file magic and big-endian header fields, with research notes throughout aimed at readers new to reverse-engineering binary formats.

title: Linux on the Atari Jaguar
blurb: Someone ported Linux to the Atari Jaguar, exploiting the console's Motorola 68000 and m68k's MMU-less uClinux support.

title: Google has introduced new rules for Chrome extensions
blurb: Google is updating its Chrome Web Store Developer Program Policies to tighten data collection, ban predictive-market extensions, and prohibit extensions built to bypass the safety guardrails of AI-powered services. Under the revised Limited Use and Disclosure rules, extensions may only collect data strictly necessary to their single disclosed purpose and must prominently surface all collection, with enforcement beginning August 1, 2026.

title: OpenSSH 10.4/10.4p1 Released
blurb: OpenSSH 10.4 adds experimental support for a composite post-quantum signature scheme combining ML-DSA 44 and Ed25519, generated via `ssh-keygen -t mldsa44-ed25519` and off by default. The release also patches several client-side flaws, including SFTP downloads that malicious servers could redirect, SCP remote-to-remote copies that could write to parent directories, and a use-after-free during key reexchange when a server changes host keys.

title: OfficeCLI: Office suite for AI agents to read and edit Microsoft Office files
blurb: OfficeCLI is an open-source, single-binary CLI that gives AI coding agents read and edit access to Word, Excel, and PowerPoint files without an Office install or dependencies. Its built-in HTML rendering engine renders .docx, .xlsx, and .pptx to HTML or PNG so the agent can inspect its own output and close the render-look-fix loop, with an install skill for Claude Code, Cursor, and Windsurf.

title: Gemini Code Assist will shut down on July 17
blurb: Google is sunsetting the consumer version of Gemini Code Assist on GitHub, with installations blocked starting June 18 and all automated code review activity ending July 17. The enterprise version is unaffected, so teams relying on the free consumer app for PR reviews will need to migrate to the paid tier or swap in another tool before the shutdown date.

title: Accessibility is the first-class interface for AI agents
blurb: The author argues that AI browser agents should navigate through the accessibility tree rather than screenshots, since the web has exposed a machine-readable interface of roles, labels, and states for decades. Switching from screenshot-based to DOM-native execution cut per-action latency from 2-5 seconds to under 500ms and dropped token cost by an order of magnitude, though the tree still handles discovery, not execution contracts.

title: Rust language rises to top 10 in Tiobe popularity index
blurb: Rust cracked the top 10 in Tiobe's programming language popularity index for the first time, ranking 10th at a 1.34% rating in the July 2026 edition. Tiobe attributed the climb to Rust's memory safety guarantees and fast codegen, positioning it against C and C++, which sit second and third but continue to draw scrutiny over manual memory management.

title: CS2 Fog Of War: Server-sided anti-wallhack occlusion culling for CS2 servers
blurb: CS2FOW is a Metamod server plugin that fights wallhacks by withholding hidden enemy entity data at the transmit layer, so any enemy fully occluded by solid map geometry never reaches the client for a cheat to read. It swaps per-tick TraceRay checks for a pre-baked BVH8 visibility matrix refreshed on a background worker thread, cutting a 12v12 worst case from around 60ms to roughly 1ms average with 8ms peaks.

title: Event-driven plumbing. When plain SQS + Lambda beats EventBridge Pipes
blurb: The author argues that for a simple "SQS to Lambda to SQS" pipeline, EventBridge Pipes aren't worth the $0.40 per million requests or the coarser failure handling, since existing serverless primitives already do the job better. The post walks through the trade-off, noting Pipes shine for low-code HTTP API enrichment on an event stream but lose their edge for plumbing your own Lambda-based enrichment between queues.

title: On the risk of coding before testing: An empirical study on LLM-based test generation workflow
blurb: This paper challenges the assumption behind test-first and agentic LLM workflows: that a model's generated tests act as independent oracles. The authors show error propagation is prevalent, with faulty code biasing subsequent test generation and dropping fault-detection rates from 25% to 14%, since incorrect implementations and their tests become mutually consistent and mask the defect rather than expose it.

title: AI Agent Pull Requests on GitHub: Frequency, Structure, and Merge Conflict Rates
blurb: This paper analyzes 33,596 agent-authored pull requests across 2,807 GitHub repositories and finds that concurrent agent PRs are common, with co-active pairs accounting for 95% of all agent PRs within a one-week window. Merging two PRs from different agents produced textual conflicts 41.7% of the time versus 19.8% for same-agent pairs, and 84.4% of conflicted files were source code rather than dependency manifests.

title: Why low-latency Java still requires discipline?
blurb: Low-latency Java demands manual discipline around GC pauses, allocation, and warmup, since the JVM's defaults optimize for throughput over predictable tail latency.

title: AI has torched the market for junior programmers
blurb: Developer employment for workers aged 22 to 25 is down 19% from its late-2022 peak while every cohort over 30 grew, with entry-level postings off 28% and CS graduates now facing 6.1% unemployment. The author argues agentic coding assistants, not ChatGPT, drove the collapse, since the decline accelerated in 2024 as tools shifted from autocompleting lines to completing tickets, and Stanford's controls rule out the ZIRP unwind and Section 174 as the cause.

title: DNSGlobe – Rust TUI to watch DNS propagate around the world
blurb: DNSGlobe is a Rust terminal UI that queries 34 public DNS resolvers worldwide in parallel, groups their answers, and plots each server's current view of a record on a world map. It queries every resolver directly with EDNS0 and TCP fallback, offers a watch mode that re-polls until propagation completes, and a `--once` flag that prints results and exits for scripting.

title: PRX Part 4: Our Data Strategy
blurb: Photoroom's Part 4 on PRX details how the team built the pre-training corpus for its 7B image model: a mix of public and internal datasets, re-captioned with a VLM into long, faithful descriptions and packed into a streamable format. The core argument is that pre-training should optimize for breadth and coverage rather than aesthetics, leaning on existing quality-filtered, deduplicated sources instead of rebuilding curation from scratch. Polish is deferred to fine-tuning and preference alignment on small, tightly curated sets.

title: Automatic Upgrades: best practice features for your lakehouse tables
blurb: Databricks launched Auto Upgrades for Unity Catalog managed tables, which observes how each table's clients access it over a rolling window, verifies compatibility, and then applies best-practice features like Row Tracking through a background ALTER TABLE with no manual work. Every change is reversible per table, and once all existing tables in a schema pass verification, the feature becomes a default that new tables inherit automatically.

title: Parallel $\mathcal O(\sqrt n)$ Overhead LSD Radix Sort
blurb: Radsort, a new variant of LSD radix sort, sorts data with only O(√n) additional space while staying stable, simple, and easy to parallelize. Conventional out-of-place LSD radix sort needs a full second copy of the array, so the memory savings matter most for large in-memory sorts inside analytics engines. Above roughly 2 MiB, Radsort beats the standard out-of-place approach on throughput, making the space win effectively free at the sizes data pipelines actually hit.

title: Variance Reduction Below the Randomization Grain
blurb: Instacart's data science team tackles a marketplace experimentation problem: interference between orders forces coarse cluster-level randomization, which slashes statistical power and slows down testing. The post shows that outcomes are predictable at fine grains even when treatment is assigned at the regional or switchback level, and that predictability can be exploited to cut the variance of aggregate metrics. The payoff is shorter experiments and more throughput as AI pushes build velocity past testing capacity.

title: The context engineering playbook
blurb: On the dbt Labs podcast, nao Labs co-founder Claire Gouze lays out a practical playbook for building a context layer agents can query reliably, arguing context engineering is just analytics engineering in a new medium. The job is unchanged: capture tacit business knowledge and make it structured and trustworthy, now in markdown and files rather than only dbt models. Her pragmatic point is that the biggest reliability gains come from unglamorous work, not fancy context sources, plus concrete guidance on writing evals for what you build.

title: Contextual Policies in Omnigent: Using session state to better govern AI agents
blurb: Databricks added contextual policies to Omnigent, its open-source meta-harness, letting governance rules track what an agent session has already done before allowing the next action. The session state enables per-task spending caps and guardrails that tighten as risk accumulates, and applies across any wrapped agent including Claude Code and Codex.

title: Migrating Counter Service storage: Design choices and learnings
blurb: Grab migrated its Counter Service, the anti-fraud system answering time-windowed count questions at tens of thousands of QPS and roughly a billion requests per day, from a wide-column database to Aerospike. The team used the switch to decouple storage from business logic and rework the read-modify-write data model, which previously cost four network round-trips per counter event across separate 15-minute, hourly, and daily tables. The write-up covers the reader-side safety work and the gradual rollout.

title: A guide to implementing AI data pipelines
blurb: dbt's 2026 State of Analytics Engineering report found 72% of teams prioritize AI-assisted agentic coding for data work, but only 24% prioritize AI for pipeline management. The author argues the gap is about accessibility: AI coding is self-driven and individual, while wiring agents into production orchestration is a team-level effort that needs approvals and alignment. The takeaway is that AI layers over the existing ELT stack rather than replacing it, the same way dbt sits over the warehouse.

title: Eiger: An Efficient Library for GPU-based Data Analytics
blurb: Eiger is a GPU data analytics library that adds runtime workload adaptivity, offering multiple implementation variants per operator and using lightweight statistics like HyperLogLog++ sketches to pick implementations, tune knobs, and compress data mid-query. Across the full TPC-H benchmark at scale factor 100, it cuts total runtime by up to 1.8x versus cuDF, with individual queries running up to 6.1x faster.

title: Show HN: Pulpie – Models for Cleaning the Web
blurb: Pulpie, an open-source family of models for extracting main content from HTML, hit Hugging Face, with the 210M-parameter pulpie-orange-small scoring 0.862 ROUGE-5 F1 on WebMainBench against leading extractor Dripper's 0.864 at a third the size. On an NVIDIA L4, it cleans 1 billion pages for $7,900 versus Dripper's $159,000, a 20x cost cut aimed at pre-training corpora and inference-time context.

title: TabPack: Efficient Hyperparameter Ensembles for Tabular Deep Learning
blurb: TabPack is an MLP ensemble for tabular deep learning that trains many networks with different hyperparameters in parallel during a single run, selecting ensemble members on the fly, so users specify sampling ranges instead of exact values. With default settings it matches extensively tuned prior methods on medium-to-large datasets while cutting the compute and effort needed. The authors report that default TabPack on a modern MacBook finished faster than tuning some baselines on an industry-grade GPU.

title: Reimagining Data Modeling on the Lakehouse: Introducing Vibe Data Modeling
blurb: Databricks introduced Vibe Data Modeling, an LLM agent that turns a plain-English business description into a governed, deployable Silver-layer data model in Unity Catalog, shipping as a single notebook with four widgets and one run. It replaces the six-month-to-three-year hand-build or the nine-to-twelve-month template rewire, using a multi-model ensemble that designs top-down and lets you refine the output in plain English until it fits.

title: Dashboard2Code: Evaluating Multimodal Models on Reconstructing Interactive Dashboards
blurb: This paper introduces Dashboard2Code, a task that has multimodal models actively click and filter through an interactive dashboard, then generate the Plotly and Dash code to reproduce it. The authors release DashboardMimic, a 180-pair benchmark across three difficulty levels and eight interaction patterns, plus an evaluation framework that combines code semantic analysis with dynamic interaction testing. Even the strongest models struggle on high-complexity dashboards, and a substantial gap remains between open- and closed-source systems.

title: Scaling Security Alert Triage With Specialized Agents on Databricks
blurb: Databricks rebuilt its security alert triage pipeline around 17 source-specific agents plus a shared threat-intelligence agent, after a single foundation model reading every alert in one prompt escalated 50% of them and produced noise rather than triage. The lesson is that accurate triage is a context problem: false-positive patterns, behavioral baselines, and enrichment are specific to each detection source, so one generalist agent lacks the grounding to tell normal from abnormal. Splitting the work by source let the team investigate low-severity alerts it previously skipped.

title: Exploiting Structural Properties for Efficient Constraint-Aware HNSW Hyperparameter Tuning
blurb: This paper tackles HNSW hyperparameter tuning, the knobs that govern the recall-latency trade-off in the vector indexes behind RAG systems. The authors show HNSW's configuration space has exploitable structure, with monotonic and unimodal relationships that let their framework, CHAT, prune resource-infeasible configs before building the index rather than treating tuning as a black box. Against strong baselines, CHAT reaches up to 45% higher throughput or 11% higher recall and converges up to 44x faster, all while respecting constraints on accuracy, latency, build time, and index size.

title: Time-Series LLMs, Explained with t0-alpha
blurb: A walkthrough of time-series foundation models, using t0-alpha, a 102M-parameter probabilistic forecaster from The Forecasting Company released in June 2026 with open Apache-2.0 weights. The model patches a numerical sequence, runs it through a causal transformer, and emits quantiles rather than point forecasts. The author reproduced its GIFT-Eval headline numbers exactly (0.4941 CRPS, 0.7240 MASE), and argues the usual foundation-model intuition is weaker here since time series lack the shared structure that text and images have.

title: GORIO: GPU-Centered Remote I/O for Graph ANNS over NVMe-oF
blurb: GORIO is a GPU-centered remote I/O substrate for graph-based approximate nearest neighbor search over NVMe-oF, keeping query evolution, page-miss generation, and resume decisions on the GPU while the CPU acts only as a transport proxy. On a SIFT1M DiskANN-style workload, it runs 1.31x faster than the state-of-the-art remote-I/O path and 3.73x faster than direct remote page-cache access. The bet is that when the next data access is discovered inside GPU traversal, the CPU should stop gating when compute resumes.

title: Meet Kirki: WordPress’s First Visual Builder With An Infinite Canvas
blurb: Kirki is a no-code visual builder for WordPress built around a freeform infinite canvas, with an integrated CMS and built-in features that aim to replace the usual stack of third-party plugins. The free WordPress.org version is the notable part, offering full infinite-canvas building rather than a stripped teaser, though this Smashing Magazine review is a sponsored piece and reads accordingly.

title: Studio Blackburn gives Meat Ellis Butchers a playful identity split across two Blackheath shops
blurb: Studio Blackburn created a split identity for Meat Ellis Butchers, giving founder Nick Ellis's two Blackheath shops distinct looks while a shared cast of smiling meat characters and a stacked sans-serif wordmark tie them under one parent brand. The historic Village location gets racing-green elegance with serif type and traditional tiling, while the younger Standard shop runs loud with bright orange, chunky type, and cheeky illustration.

title: The promotion that didn’t take
blurb: The author argues that design seniority is not one measurement but two independent axes, scope and operating maturity, that the industry has forced into a single title for twenty years. Building a career assessment for designers, they found nearly half the people they measured diverged from their badge, with senior scope masking junior operating behavior like treating feedback as a threat and restarting every project from zero.

title: Apple overhauls RAW photo processing with iOS 27, showcases impressive results
blurb: Apple introduced RAW 9 in iOS 27, macOS 27, and iPadOS 27, a system-level image pipeline that fuses demosaicing and denoising in a single tiled CoreML model running on the Neural Engine. The engine sharpens fine detail and cuts luma and chroma noise on both new captures and reprocessed older RAW files, spanning Apple's calibrations for nearly 800 third-party camera models.

title: Why a bitten ‘t' from 1889 was the inspiration for Fatype's States
blurb: Fatype's States, a 28-style sans family from designers Yassin Baggar and Anton Koovit, traces its origins to a lowercase 't' with a bite taken out of its ascender, found only in the 48-point size of Boston Type Foundry's 1889 Gothic N° 2 specimen. The typeface treats letterpress imperfections as design features, reviving the rough, unsettled character of American Gothics before Morris Fuller Benton standardized them into tidy families.

title: Casul chair unfolds to become a children's play set
blurb: Irish furniture designer James Murphy won a top prize at London's New Designers showcase for Casul, a plywood children's chair that unfolds into a castle-shaped play set in two moves. Built around timber joints rather than hardware, the piece shifts from a compact miniature throne to an open play structure, a nod to furniture that earns its footprint by serving more than one function.

title: Sporting CP unveils 1940s-informed rebrand that "stays faithful to the club's legacy"
blurb: Sporting Clube de Portugal released an updated badge and identity from Jones Knowles Ritchie, drawing on the club's 1940s crest to mark its 120th anniversary. JKR tightened the lion, shield, and ball into a cleaner, more legible mark built to scale across kits and digital, while framing the work as continuity with the club's history rather than a break from it.

title: Giannone Petricone Associates "weaves" glass addition into 1820s Toronto house
blurb: Giannone Petricone Associates renovated Snider House, North Toronto's oldest surviving structure, pairing the 1828 brick Regency cottage with a wavy glass addition that curves against the orthogonal original. The Toronto studio kept the farmhouse's ordered geometry legible while a contemporary interior and the undulating extension mark the new work, a restrained take on the old-meets-new grafting that usually reads as pastiche.

title: Elon Musk’s new SpaceXAI logo looks surprisingly familiar
blurb: Elon Musk merged xAI into SpaceX under a single brand, SpaceXAI, retiring the contentious 2023 xAI mark for a logo that fuses SpaceX's swoop with xAI's angular structure. The identity has drawn immediate pushback from designers over both the smooshed phonetics of the name and a wordmark that many say reads less like a frontier tech brand and more like a Reebok throwback.

title: Carlo Ratti and Park Associati to surround children's hospital in one-kilometre "green ring"
blurb: Carlo Ratti Associati and Park Associati won the commission to overhaul Brescia's Spedali Civili hospital, adding a three-pronged wing wrapped in a one-kilometre park the architects call a "green ring." The scheme merges healthcare, education, and landscape into a single site, part of a broader push to treat greenery as clinical infrastructure rather than decorative framing around medical buildings.

title: The Skibidi Toilet legal battle is a lesson for digital creators (and Hollywood studios)
blurb: Skibidi Toilet creator Alexey Gerasimov signed away primary rights to his 47M-subscriber series, including trademarks and channel control, to Invisible Narratives in 2023, and is now sidelined as a Michael Bay film deal and multimillion-dollar rights fight play out. The case is a warning for independent creators about copyright assignment terms, arriving as studios race to option viral YouTube IP with crossover potential.

title: Identifying Deceptive Patterns Across Three Age Groups: A Heuristic-Based Cognitive Walkthrough Study of Mobile Apps
blurb: This paper runs a heuristic-based cognitive walkthrough of 30 mobile apps across six categories to map how deceptive patterns are tailored to teens, adults, and older adults, finding that 93% rely on nagging and that entertainment apps carry the heaviest load. Most notable for designers: entertainment apps aimed at older adults use sneaking patterns more often than those for teens or adults, which the authors argue makes the case for age-specific ethical design guidelines.

title: Bugatti designs porcelain-clad sports car informed by Enzo Mari vase
blurb: Bugatti released the Mistral Blanc Éternal, a sports car finished with porcelain speaker grilles and armrests informed by a white vase Enzo Mari designed for the marque in the 1990s. The car carries over the vase's flowing royal-blue lines, extending the modernist designer's ceramic language onto an automotive body and pushing porcelain into structural interior surfaces it has rarely touched.

title: Ingenious Lego billboards show that sometimes showing the product is all that's needed
blurb: Lego turned two Shanghai metro stations into exhibition spaces displaying all 4,104 pieces of its new Technic Koenigsegg Sadair's Spear set, mounting the bricks across lightboxes at Lujiazui Station and West Nanjing Road rather than showing a rendered image. The campaign, which took 15 people over 3,000 hours to build, bets that the disassembled product itself is the strongest visual, breaking down the hypercar's aerodynamics and engine in brick form.

title: How two filmmakers built a 300-shot VFX pipeline without a Hollywood studio
blurb: Two filmmakers at German studio Loreley Productions built a 300-shot VFX pipeline for their Star Trek fan film Erstkontakt using Unreal Engine, DaVinci Resolve, and Blackmagic Fusion, handled entirely in post by a team of two. Their throughput comes from refusing to overcomplicate: no custom naming conventions, node trees copied shot to shot with swapped loaders, and Fusion Connect clips that push finished comps straight back into the Resolve timeline with zero local render time.

title: Apple's Liquid Glass is Having Problems… Again
blurb: Apple's Liquid Glass design language is drawing fresh criticism in the iOS 27 public beta, where users noticed that content behind the translucent panels renders at noticeably lower resolution. Some attribute the blur to Apple dropping a Gaussian pass to save GPU and RAM, and with the official release still slated for September, it remains unclear whether the company will treat it as a bug worth fixing.

title: Otherway brings Cowshed back to its Somerset roots with wild botanical illustrations
blurb: Otherway rebranded Cowshed with wild botanical illustrations that reconnect the skincare label to its rural Somerset origins at Soho House's Babington House. The refresh trades the category's minimal-typography-and-muted-tones playbook for illustrative maximalism, betting that visual warmth will differentiate Cowshed against Aesop, Byredo, and a crowd of newcomers on the same bathroom shelf.

title: 16-Year-Old Linux KVM Flaw Lets Guest VMs Escape to Host on Intel and AMD x86 Systems
blurb: A use-after-free in Linux KVM's shadow MMU, dubbed Januscape (CVE-2026-53359), lets a guest with root and nested virtualization enabled corrupt the host kernel's shadow-page state and escape to the host on both Intel and AMD x86. The 16-year-old bug was fixed in mainline on June 19, 2026; the public PoC only panics the host, but the researcher claims a private exploit achieves full host code execution.

title: New Januscape Linux flaw allows VM escape on Intel, AMD devices
blurb: Januscape (CVE-2026-53359), a 16-year-old use-after-free in KVM/x86 shadow MMU emulation, allows an attacker with root inside a guest VM to escape to the host and run code as root or panic the host kernel, taking down every co-tenant VM. It is the first guest-to-host escape triggerable on both Intel and AMD, was patched in June 2026, and multi-tenant KVM hosts should confirm commit 81ccda30b4e8 is applied.

title: Linux Kernel Vulnerability Allows VM Escape on Intel and AMD Systems
blurb: A use-after-free in the KVM hypervisor's shadow MMU code (CVE-2026-53359, "Januscape") allows a guest with root privileges to escape the VM and gain code execution on the host, the first KVM escape triggerable on both Intel and AMD. It was patched in mainline on June 19 after sitting dormant for 16 years; multi-tenant x86 clouds exposing nested virtualization should prioritize the fix, as exploitation enables host takeover or DoS across all co-tenant VMs.

title: JadePuffer: The First Complete LLM-Driven Ransomware Attack
blurb: Sysdig documented JadePuffer, the first fully autonomous LLM-driven ransomware operation, which exploited CVE-2025-3248, an unauthenticated RCE in Internet-facing Langflow, then pivoted to a production MySQL and Alibaba Nacos server to enumerate, exfiltrate, and delete the database before dropping an extortion note. No individual technique was novel, but the LLM chained credential harvesting, lateral movement, and destruction end-to-end without operator intervention, so patch Langflow and treat exposed AI-app tooling as an initial-access vector.

title: Sysdig clocks first documented case of agentic ransomware
blurb: Sysdig documented the first known case of agentic ransomware, in which an AI agent driven by threat actor JadePuffer ran an end-to-end extortion operation spanning reconnaissance, credential theft, lateral movement, persistence, encryption, and ransom-note delivery. Initial access came via CVE-2025-3248 in Langflow, and the agent fired over 600 purposeful payloads, once fixing and redeploying a broken Nacos backdoor 31 seconds after hitting an error.

title: Max severity Adobe ColdFusion flaw now exploited in attacks
blurb: Attackers are exploiting a maximum-severity Adobe ColdFusion flaw (CVE-2026-48282) that lets unauthenticated attackers achieve remote code execution on unpatched systems running versions 2025.9, 2023.20, and earlier. KEVIntel observed in-the-wild exploitation within two hours of Adobe's disclosure, and with nearly 800 instances exposed online, admins should apply the patch immediately per Adobe's 72-hour guidance.

title: AI agent exploits Langflow in first fully autonomous ransomware attack
blurb: Sysdig documented JadePuffer, which it calls the first ransomware campaign run end-to-end by an autonomous LLM agent, breaking in via CVE-2025-3248, a critical (CVSS 9.8) Langflow flaw added to CISA's KEV in May 2025. The agent dumped Langflow's PostgreSQL database for cloud and AI-service credentials, then encrypted 1,342 Nacos config items with a random key, making recovery impossible even if the Bitcoin ransom is paid.

title: Januscape: Guest-to-Host Escape in KVM/x86 [CVE-2026-53359]
blurb: Researcher Hyunwoo Kim disclosed Januscape (CVE-2026-53359), a use-after-free in KVM/x86 shadow MMU emulation that lets a guest corrupt the host kernel's shadow page and escape to the host, triggerable on both Intel and AMD. The bug threatens multi-tenant clouds exposing nested virtualization, was used as a 0-day in Google kvmCTF, and had lain dormant for roughly 16 years before the June 2026 fix.

title: North Korean Hackers Target Open Source Developers in Supply Chain Attacks
blurb: North Korean threat actors are running PolinRider, a supply-chain campaign active since December 2025 that compromises maintainer accounts across NPM, Packagist, Go modules, and Chrome extensions to push the DEV#POPPER RAT and OmniStealer, per Socket. Socket has found 162 malicious artifacts across 108 packages, with attackers rewriting Git history to age malicious commits, so teams that installed any affected version should remediate developer credentials from a clean machine.

title: BeyondTrust Patches Critical Auth Bypass Flaws in Remote Support and PRA
blurb: BeyondTrust patched four flaws in Remote Support and Privileged Remote Access, including two critical pre-auth bypasses (CVE-2026-40138 and CVE-2026-40139, both CVSS 9.2) that let network-positioned attackers reach appliance accounts with elevated privileges. Exploitation of both hinges on a specific authentication configuration being enabled, but given these appliances gate privileged access, operators should patch immediately and audit for the vulnerable config.

title: BeyondTrust warns of critical flaws in remote access software
blurb: BeyondTrust disclosed two critical authentication-bypass flaws (CVE-2026-40138 and CVE-2026-40139) in its Remote Support and Privileged Remote Access products, letting unauthenticated attackers reach appliances and elevated-privilege accounts under specific auth configurations. Cloud instances were patched April 21, but self-hosted customers must upgrade to RS/PRA 25.3.3 or apply the April rollup; Shadowserver tracks nearly 2,000 exposed instances online.

title: CERT/CC Warns of Hidden Admin Backdoor in Tenda Router Firmware
blurb: CERT/CC disclosed an undocumented authentication backdoor (CVE-2026-11405) in multiple Tenda router firmware versions, where the httpd login() function falls back to a plaintext comparison against a hidden "rzadmin" password to grant unauthenticated admin access (role=2) with any username. The flaw is unpatched, so operators running affected AC5, AC6, AC10, W15E, or FH1201 devices should disable remote management and restrict LAN reachability until a fix ships.

title: When Claws Remember but Do Not Tell: Stealthy Memory Injection in Persistent Personal Agents
