blurb: This paper introduces stealth memory injection, an attack in which a single untrusted email induces a persistent personal agent to silently write poisoned data into long-term memory, hide it from the user, and act on it later. The authors' one-shot MemGhost framework hit 87.5% end-to-end success against OpenClaw on GPT-5.4 and 71.4% against Claude Code SDK on Sonnet 4.6, transferring across memory backends and defeating input-, model-, and system-level defenses.

title: Fake IT bods on Microsoft Teams coax workers into installing malware
blurb: Attackers are impersonating internal IT staff over Microsoft Teams messages and calls, using social engineering to walk targeted employees through installing remote-access tooling that delivers malware. Defenders should restrict external Teams federation and inbound chats from untrusted tenants, enforce that legitimate IT never requests remote-support installs via unsolicited Teams contact, and alert on quick-assist or RMM binaries spawned shortly after a Teams session.

title: Fake IT support calls on Microsoft Teams push EtherRAT malware
blurb: Threat actors are impersonating IT support in Microsoft Teams voice calls to trick employees into granting remote control and installing EtherRAT, a Node.js RAT that uses Ethereum smart contracts to resolve its C2. Unit 42 traced the chain from an "Employee Survey" phishing PDF through legitimate tools HopToDesk and AnyDesk to an MSI loader, and found staged installers v1 through v9, indicating active development.

title: Threat Actors Probe Gitea Docker Flaw CVE-2026-20896 13 Days After Disclosure
blurb: Threat actors have begun probing CVE-2026-20896 (CVSS 9.8), a critical authentication bypass in Gitea Docker images whose default config trusts the X-WEBAUTH-USER header from any source IP, letting an unauthenticated client impersonate any known user, including admins. Gitea fixed it in 1.26.3 by removing the wildcard and making reverse-proxy auth opt-in; roughly 6,200 internet-facing instances remain exposed, so patch and audit for rogue admin logins.

title: Anthropic Accuses Alibaba of Claude Distillation/Cloning Attack
blurb: Anthropic accused Alibaba and its Qwen AI lab of running the largest Claude distillation campaign it has measured, generating 28.8M exchanges through roughly 25,000 fraudulent accounts between April 22 and June 5 to extract agentic reasoning and software-engineering capabilities. In a June 10 letter to senators, Anthropic said the operators used obfuscation and proxy networks, and warned that a growing "circumvention economy" is fueling further extraction attacks against US frontier models.

title: NSA and IETF: Fairness
blurb: Cryptographer Daniel Bernstein argues that an NSA-backed IETF last call to standardize solo ML-KEM in TLS should be opposed, citing declassified history of NSA weakening DES, RC4, RSA-512, and RNG standards to keep them exploitable. He contends solo post-quantum key exchange invites predictable implementation bugs, and that hybrid ECC+ML-KEM already carries most of Cloudflare's PQ traffic; the mailing-list vote closes 2026-07-08.

title: Prompt Injection Attacks Trick AI Agents Into Making Crypto Payments
blurb: Zscaler identified two campaigns using indirect prompt injection to trick AI agents into crypto payments, including a fake requests-secure-v2 Python library that hides payment instructions in schema markup and a typosquatted DeBank clone. In Zscaler's tests, four of 26 evaluated LLMs, including Llama 3.3 70B and Gemini 2.5 Pro, followed the injected instructions.

title: Suspected China-Aligned Hackers Exploit Roundcube Flaws Against Universities
blurb: Suspected China-aligned cluster UNK_MassTraction is exploiting patched Roundcube webmail flaws, including CVE-2024-42009 (CVSS 9.3), against physics and engineering departments at U.S. and Canadian universities to steal credentials, 2FA tokens, and cookies. The XSS chain triggers on email open and delivers the IceCube stealer, then deploys a web shell or VShell for persistence, so patch Roundcube to a non-N-day version and rotate exposed credentials.

title: Your Agent's Memories Are Not Its Own: Forged Reasoning Attacks on LLM Agent Memory and Defenses
blurb: This paper introduces FARMA (Forged Amplifying Rationale Memory Attack), which poisons an LLM agent's stored reasoning history rather than its factual memory, using evasive phrasing to bypass keyword filters and self-referential reinforcement to defeat consensus defenses, reaching up to 100% attack success and beating A-MemGuard. The authors' SENTINEL defense structurally analyzes memory entries for forgery via five weighted signals, cutting attack success to 0% with no false positives across 326 benign traces.

title: Proof-of-Concept Exploit Released for Linux ‘Bad Epoll’ Root Access Vulnerability
blurb: A public PoC exploits Bad Epoll (CVE-2026-46242, CVSS 7.8), a close-vs-close race-condition use-after-free in the Linux kernel's epoll file-release path that gives unprivileged processes root via a ROP chain. Distributions on kernel 6.4 or newer are affected, including Pixel 10 devices on 6.6; the correct maintainer patch landed two months after disclosure, so prioritize kernel updates on exposed hosts.

title: OpenWrt One – Open Hardware Router
blurb: The OpenWrt project released OpenWrt One, a fully open-hardware router designed to run OpenWrt firmware out of the box with no vendor lock-in or proprietary blobs in its core design. For security teams, it offers an auditable, community-maintained network appliance where firmware provenance and update control stay entirely in the operator's hands rather than a vendor's.

title: Untrusted Content Masking for Web Agents with Security Guarantees
blurb: This paper introduces Untrusted Content Masking (UCM), a defense that restores the trusted/untrusted boundary web agents lose when they render pages mixing both. UCM uses the DOM to distinguish trusted from untrusted regions without reading their content, redacting untrusted regions before they reach the agent and routing interaction through a sandboxed interface with strict privilege separation. The approach aims to extend provable prompt-injection defenses, which depend on strict isolation, to browsing agents. Code is available.

title: Agent Data Injection Attacks are Realistic Threats to AI Agents
blurb: A new paper introduces agent data injection (ADI), a class of indirect prompt injection that disguises malicious input as trusted metadata like resource identifiers or tool-call formats, bypassing existing instruction-injection defenses. The authors demonstrate arbitrary-click attacks against web agents (Claude in Chrome, Antigravity, Nanobrowser) and RCE plus supply-chain compromise against coding agents (Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI), tracing the root cause to agents failing to isolate trusted from untrusted data.

title: BonkDAO loses $20 million following ‘malicious governance proposal’ attack
blurb: BonkDAO lost $20 million after an attacker pushed through a malicious governance proposal, draining funds from the BONK protocol's treasury. The stolen assets have been traced to centralized exchanges, and South Korea's Upbit suspended BONK deposits and withdrawals in an attempt to freeze the outflow.

title: France to Stop Certifying Non-Quantum-Safe Encryption
blurb: France's cybersecurity agency ANSSI will stop certifying security products that lack quantum-resistant encryption starting in 2027, and advised businesses to purchase only quantum-safe products by 2030. Because ANSSI approval is required for French government agencies and critical infrastructure, the policy functions as a de facto phase-out of pre-quantum cryptography across the country's most sensitive systems.

title: Suspected Chinese espionage group used a Roundcube exploit chain to burrow into universities
blurb: Suspected Chinese espionage cluster UNK_MassTraction chained two Roundcube webmail flaws, CVE-2024-42009 and CVE-2025-49113, to steal credentials and plant webshells across U.S. and Canadian universities, Proofpoint reported. The initial exploit fires when a victim opens a phishing email, so patch Roundcube, rotate mailserver credentials, and hunt for VShell backdoors on physics and engineering department hosts.

title: New TrojPix Attack Leaks Data From Air-Gapped Systems via Video Cable Emissions
blurb: Researchers at Shandong University disclosed TrojPix, an air-gap exfiltration technique that modulates on-screen pixels imperceptibly so the video cable radiates a decodable RF signal, hitting a peak 8.1 Mbps and 208-meter range in separate tests. It requires no admin rights or hardware changes, only user-level malware that can draw to the screen, and remains lab-stage rather than seen in the wild.

title: Iran-Linked Hackers Use New Cavern C2 Framework to Target Israeli Organizations
blurb: Check Point Research documented Cavern Manticore, an MOIS-linked cluster overlapping with MuddyWater and OilRig's Lyceum, deploying a previously undocumented modular .NET C2 framework called Cavern against Israeli IT providers and government bodies. The attack chain abuses SysAid's software update feature to trigger DLL side-loading of a trojanized uxtheme.dll, which fetches five post-exploitation modules over HTTPS or WebSocket for AD recon, SQL enumeration, SMB/LDAP brute-forcing, and SOCKS5 tunneling.

title: CertiK Says H1 2026 Web3 Losses Topped $1.31B, Up 28% Excluding Bybit Baseline
blurb: CertiK's Hack3D H1 2026 report tallied more than $1.31B in Web3 losses across 344 incidents, roughly 28% above the year-ago baseline once the outlier $1.45B Bybit hack is stripped from H1 2025's total. Wallet compromise led at $444M across 33 incidents, and CertiK flagged escalating North Korea-linked activity, most notably Lazarus Group, as a heightened concern for the second half of 2026.

title: CitrixBleed-ing Again? NetScaler Vulnerability Under Attack
blurb: Citrix disclosed CVE-2026-8451 (CVSS 8.8) on June 30, a memory-overread flaw in NetScaler ADC and Gateway devices configured as a SAML identity provider that lets a remote attacker leak sensitive data reminiscent of 2023's CitrixBleed. WatchTowr published a PoC the same day, and Lupovis observed a single threat actor exploiting the flaw from 146.70.139[.]154 within 24 hours, so patch and treat SAML IDP appliances as exposed.

title: Spain collars alleged pro-Russia hacktivist after FBI tip-off
blurb: Spanish police arrested a suspected pro-Russia hacktivist in Palencia after a tip-off from the FBI, linking the individual to attacks on government and critical-infrastructure targets. The arrest reflects continued cross-border coordination between US and European agencies against ideologically motivated actors carrying out DDoS and defacement campaigns aligned with Russian interests.

title: Microsoft wants to keep your AI agents from going rogue
blurb: Microsoft released Microsoft Execution Containers (MXC) in early preview, a policy-driven runtime layer that isolates AI agents on Windows and WSL through process and session isolation enforced via Entra and Intune. The early preview restricts AI-generated code from files and network resources and separates agents from the user's desktop, clipboard, and active sessions, with micro-VM support planned for later releases.

title: ECB Asks Banks for Plans to Address AI Cybersecurity Threats
blurb: The European Central Bank asked euro-area banks to submit plans for countering cybersecurity threats from frontier AI models such as Anthropic's Claude Mythos. The request signals that supervisors now treat offensive AI capability as a systemic operational risk, and banks should expect AI threat readiness to factor into future stress tests and DORA compliance reviews.

title: Secret Claude tracker shocks users after Anthropic’s anti-surveillance stance
blurb: Anthropic secretly added tracking code to Claude Code in March that flagged Chinese users' timezone, proxy, and possible ties to AI labs suspected of distillation attacks, using "prompt steganography" to hide it until a researcher exposed it. The company removed the tracker and framed it as an anti-abuse experiment, but the disclosure undercuts its public anti-surveillance stance and its ongoing lawsuit against the White House over Claude-based surveillance.

title: Full Writeup of the Windows GDID
blurb: A reverse-engineering writeup traces the Windows Global Device Identifier (GDID) named in the July 2026 Scattered Spider complaint to a 64-bit Microsoft Account Device PUID that wlidsvc provisions, stores in the registry, and propagates through the Connected Devices Platform to Delivery Optimization. The analysis debunks claims the ID is 128-bit or derived from hardware serials, noting the court record itself confirms a reinstall mints a new GDID, and shows defenders can read their own value from one registry key.

title: Google Is Suing Chinese Scammers Who Are Using Gemini
blurb: Google filed suit against Outsider Enterprise, a China-based group that sells phishing-as-a-service via Telegram and instructs subscribers to use Gemini to generate nearly 300 scam templates impersonating Google, YouTube, and agencies like New York's E-ZPass. Google coordinated with AT&T, Verizon, and T-Mobile to block the malicious texts, and credits on-device scam detection in Google Messages, which it says stops 10 billion scam texts monthly.

title: Measuring Healthcare Data Leaks and Security Flaws at Internet Scale
blurb: Researchers ran nine-month honeypots and Internet-wide IPv4/IPv6 scans of the three major healthcare protocols, DICOM, HL7, and FHIR, and found widespread authentication flaws exposing patient data. Many exposed systems lack transport encryption entirely and run software with known CVEs, some scored up to CVSS 9.8 with system-takeover potential. Defenders operating medical-protocol services should segment them off the public Internet, enforce TLS and authentication, and patch known-vulnerable healthcare software rather than assuming obscurity protects HL7 and FHIR endpoints.

title: Post-Quantum Migration and Abandoned Coins
blurb: Coinbase's Quantum Advisory Council outlined how the crypto ecosystem should migrate to post-quantum signature schemes before quantum computers can break the ECDSA keys securing existing wallets. The harder problem is abandoned coins: funds in wallets whose owners are lost or inactive can't be migrated by their holders, leaving exposed public keys crackable once quantum capability arrives. The council frames this as a governance question over whether to freeze or burn vulnerable coins ahead of that threshold.

title: US cyber agency is using Anthropic’s Mythos to audit government code, sources say
blurb: CISA is reportedly running Anthropic's Mythos model against government code repositories to flag vulnerabilities, with the agency's Attack Surface Evaluation team already surfacing a large number of bugs, according to three people who spoke to Reuters. None of the operational detail is confirmed on the record, and the deployment continues even as Anthropic and the White House spar over safeguards blocking the dual-use model from autonomous weapons and domestic surveillance.

title: Ghost Traffic: ICMP Tunneling-Based Billing Bypass in LTE Networks
blurb: Ghost Traffic is an Android system that abuses cellular ISPs' practice of excluding ICMP echo traffic from data billing, using VpnService to encapsulate all app traffic into ICMP echo payloads and route it through an external proxy without root privileges. Researchers confirmed end-to-end billing bypass in six of seven ISP environments across South Korea, Japan, and the US, with no QoS throttling applied even after the data cap was exhausted.

title: Blogspot-Hosted Payloads Delivered in ‘Veil#Drop’ Attacks
blurb: Securonix disclosed Veil#Drop, a multi-stage malware framework that abuses Blogspot on Google's trusted infrastructure to host payloads, chaining JavaScript launchers, PowerShell cradles, XOR-encoded .NET assemblies, and LOLBIN abuse to deliver PureLog Stealer via fileless in-memory execution. The chain starts with a document-masquerading JavaScript file and ends in browser credential, cookie, session-token, and crypto-wallet theft, so defenders should hunt for PowerShell spawned from script files and outbound traffic to Blogspot-hosted pages.

title: Why sandboxing your agent is not enough
blurb: The author argues that sandboxing an AI agent is necessary but insufficient once you run agents at scale on Kubernetes, and compares two projects that address different gaps. Agent-sandbox (a Kubernetes SIG Apps CRD and controller) supplies strong agent identities, persistent storage, and lifecycle-managed isolated pods, while the newer agent-substrate decouples the agent "actor" from short-lived worker pods that suspend when idle and resume on any available worker, trading always-on containers for higher density and lower latency without giving up sandboxed execution.

title: Armored Likho APT Targeting Government, Electric Power Entities
blurb: Kaspersky detailed Armored Likho, a newly tracked APT running spear-phishing campaigns against government and electric power organizations in Russia, Brazil, and Kazakhstan, using LNK and executable lures that fetch malware from GitHub repositories in the background. Its Python-based BusySnake Stealer harvests browser credentials, cookies, OTP keys, crypto wallets, and Telegram sessions, and establishes reverse SSH tunnels for persistent control, so defenders should hunt for anomalous GitHub archive fetches and unexpected RustDesk activity.

title: Retroactive Chain-of-Thought (RetroCoT): Forensic Reconstruction Prompts as a Safety Diagnostic Across Model Generations
blurb: This paper introduces Retroactive Chain-of-Thought (RetroCoT), a single-turn jailbreak that reframes harmful requests as forensic reconstruction tasks and achieves 58% attack success on gpt-4o versus a 0% direct-request baseline. GPT-5-family models refuse the base technique, but a single adversarial follow-up turn extending the forensic frame lifts success to 48% on GPT-5.4-mini and 94% on gpt-4o, showing alignment remains conditioned on pragmatic framing rather than semantic intent.

title: Summer Finance Drained of $6M in Flash Loan Exploit
blurb: Summer Finance (SummerFi) was drained of roughly $6M when an attacker used a $65.4M flash loan to abuse the Fleet Commander vault's trust in its Ark strategy contract, inflating reported assets to ~$7.14M and redeeming the difference in a single transaction. The exploit relied on legitimate deposit, redeem, and withdrawFromArks calls rather than compromised keys; the team paused all Lazy Summer vaults while investigating the accounting flaw.

title: The ‘first’ AI-run ransomware attack still needed a human
blurb: Sysdig documented JadePuffer, which it calls the first "agentic ransomware" case, in which an AI agent autonomously chained a known Langflow bug and a MySQL flaw to gain admin, moved laterally, encrypted over 1,300 config records, and wrote its own ransom note. A human still chose the victim, provisioned the C2 and staging infrastructure, and supplied credentials from a prior compromise, so the operation was AI-executed but human-directed.

title: 'BusySnake' Infostealer Slithers into Critical Infrastructure Networks
blurb: Kaspersky disclosed a previously unknown APT it tracks as "Armored Likho" targeting government agencies and electrical-power entities in Russia, Brazil, and Kazakhstan via spear-phishing that masquerades as official government or social-assistance communications. The campaign delivers "BusySnake," an undocumented Python infostealer that harvests credentials and sensitive documents, and Kaspersky notes redundant comments in the loaders suggest the group is using LLMs to generate first-stage payloads.

title: Keyfactor Scores $1 Billion+ Investment for AI, Post-Quantum Security
blurb: Keyfactor raised a strategic growth investment exceeding $1B, led by Summit Partners, to expand its Trust Control Plane platform for post-quantum cryptography and machine-identity management. The round targets enterprises facing identity sprawl and a 2030 federal quantum-safe migration deadline set by the White House's June 2026 executive order, with Keyfactor currently managing billions of machine identities for over 2,500 organizations.

title: Google hobbles NetNut proxy infrastructure amid botnet crackdown
blurb: Google, working with Lumen and the FBI, disrupted NetNut (aka Popa), a residential proxy network of at least two million hijacked devices including smart TVs and streaming boxes, by disabling the Google accounts and services it used for malware command and control. Google reported more than 300 distinct threat clusters using NetNut exit nodes in a single week of June for espionage and intrusion, and said the takedown degraded the network by millions of devices.

title: New Java-Based QuimaRAT MaaS Built to Run on Windows, Linux, and macOS
blurb: Researchers detailed QuimaRAT, a new Java-based cross-platform RAT sold as a MaaS from $150/month to $1,200 for lifetime access, with a modular architecture that loads encrypted plugins from C2 and targets Windows, Linux, and macOS. Its builder generates JAR, EXE, APP, SH, BAT, and VBS payloads, and a companion Quima Loader stages EXEs behind fake CAPTCHA or update lures.

title: From Multiplicity to Vulnerability: Privacy Amplification Risk from One-Dataset-Multiple-Model Exposure
blurb: This paper shows that reusing one dataset to train multiple task-specific models (facial recognition, age estimation, race classification) served as separate APIs substantially amplifies privacy leakage, a phenomenon the authors term ODMM privacy composition. Their PRIME attack aggregates membership-inference signals across the exposed models and consistently outperforms single-task MIAs across five image and text benchmarks and architectures from ResNet to Qwen3-1.7B. Differential privacy reduces but does not eliminate the leakage, so teams reusing sensitive datasets should treat cumulative per-model exposure as a design constraint.

title: RustMizan: A Compilable, Contamination-Aware Benchmarking Framework for Rust Vulnerabilities
blurb: RustMizan is a benchmarking framework for Rust vulnerability analysis that provides compilable code at crate, file, and function levels, with annotations for binary detection, CWE classification, and function- and line-level localization plus semantics-preserving mutants for contamination testing. Across four frontier models in an agentic setup, binary classification hit 56-65%, but line localization F1 stayed near 20% and dropped about 27% under adversarial cues.

title: Let My Data Go: Data Brokers' Compliance with Opt-Out and Deletion Requests
blurb: Researchers submitted CCPA opt-out and deletion requests to every California-registered data broker using synthetic identities, and found that while most complied, a significant fraction ignored requests or failed to acknowledge them. Some brokers demanded intrusive identity verification the CCPA explicitly prohibits, and the fragmented, high-effort submission process across brokers leaves consumers with little practical recourse. The authors call for a standardized request interface, stronger enforcement, and real penalties for sustained non-compliance.

title: Beyond Compliance: A Large Scale Study on the Completeness and Consistency of the GitHub SBOMs
blurb: A large-scale study of 10,000 GitHub repositories across ten language ecosystems found that GitHub's auto-generated SBOMs are not NTIA-compliant, though core metadata is consistently present. Component version and license coverage varies sharply by ecosystem, so teams relying on GitHub SBOMs for vulnerability or license analysis should validate against tools like Syft or Trivy. The authors found GitHub matches the Microsoft SBOM Tool and often outperforms Syft and Trivy on version and license data.

title: Microsoft Can Track Users via a Windows Device ID
blurb: Court documents from a hacker's arrest revealed that Microsoft can tie a Windows device to a user through a persistent hardware-linked device ID that ties telemetry and account activity back to specific machines. The disclosure shows how much identifying signal Windows sends by default, giving defenders and privacy teams reason to review telemetry settings and device-ID exposure on managed fleets.

title: OpenSSH 10.4/10.4p1 Released
blurb: OpenSSH 10.4/10.4p1 shipped, deprecating the DSA signature algorithm entirely and disabling the finite-field Diffie-Hellman group1 key exchange by default. The release also hardens ssh-agent against confused-deputy attacks and fixes a bug that could cause the client to skip host-key verification under specific multiplexing conditions, so operators running SSH bastions should prioritize the upgrade.

title: The Blind Spot in the Watchtower: Detections for When Someone Attacks Your Sentinel
blurb: Most SOC detections point outward at endpoints, identities, and cloud services, leaving Microsoft Sentinel itself largely unmonitored even though it's the first thing a capable attacker reaches for. This post details detections that make Sentinel watch itself, pulling from AzureActivity, SentinelAudit, and SentinelHealth to flag disabled analytics rules, paused data connectors, shortened retention, and anomalous sign-ins to the security portal. All three sources are free once the health-and-audit feature is enabled.

title: Phishing poses as big-brand job interview to steal Google accounts
blurb: A phishing campaign impersonating over 30 major brands, including Adobe, Netflix, and OpenAI, targets marketing professionals with fake job-interview lures to steal Google account credentials. The operation abuses legitimate services PeopleForce, Salesforce Marketing Cloud (exct[.]net), and Wise Agent in nested redirects to evade detection, and has been running for at least five months using real recruiters' names and photos.

title: Researchers make the case for a cybersecurity AI scientist
blurb: Researchers at the Chinese Academy of Sciences published a paper defining a "Cybersecurity AI Scientist," an autonomous system that runs the full research loop from question to experimental design, tool building, execution, and written result. They propose Hephaestus, a modular multi-agent system with role-specialized agents for problem framing, threat modeling, tool generation, and reporting, arguing that adaptive targets and drifting tooling break the assumptions behind existing automated-research systems.

title: PiSAs: Benchmarking Contextual Integrity in Multi-User Agentic Systems
blurb: This paper introduces PiSAs, a benchmark for measuring cross-user privacy spillage in shared multi-user LLM agent systems, where sensitive data can leak not just through outputs but through inter-agent messages and shared memory. Using dual contextual-integrity annotations for task-appropriateness and per-user access rights, the authors find that even state-of-the-art models fail to reliably filter inappropriate content or restrict it to authorized users, meaning architectural controls alone won't stop internal data leakage.

title: Suspected China-Nexus Hackers Use Fake Indian Tax Filing Utility to Deploy DcRAT
blurb: Suspected China-nexus actors are running Operation DragonReturn, a spear-phishing campaign impersonating India's Income Tax Department to deliver DcRAT during the country's tax filing season. The chain uses a fake offline tax utility to sideload a malicious DLL (nvdaHelperRemote.dll), extract a payload hidden in a JPG, escalate via a UAC prompt, and persist through a MixedSvc Windows service.

title: EU urged to act after Pegasus infects phone of spyware inquiry MEP
blurb: Pegasus spyware infected the phone of an MEP serving on the European Parliament committee investigating spyware abuse, prompting renewed calls for the EU to enforce restrictions on commercial surveillance vendors. The compromise of an oversight body's own member underscores the bloc's failure to act on prior inquiry recommendations, leaving journalists, activists, and officials exposed to mercenary spyware across member states.

title: Zscaler finds autonomous agents succumb to IPI traps
blurb: Zscaler tested 26 LLMs against indirect prompt injection (IPI) embedded in websites and found four autonomous agents, including Gemini-2.5-pro and two Llama3 variants, took inappropriate actions on hidden malicious instructions that would not fool a human. Notably, some cheaper models resisted better than pricier ones. Treat web content as an attack surface for agents, and don't rely on a static safe/vulnerable label, since agent behavior shifts as inputs change.

title: US Army websites defaced with pro-Kurdish sentiments, insults to Trump
blurb: Multiple U.S. Army subdomains, including oil.army.mil and ai2c.army.mil, were defaced in a 404 hijacking campaign that swapped error pages for pro-Kurdish messages and insults aimed at Trump. The affected WordPress sites, hosted on a legacy third-party platform, were taken offline after disclosure, though the initial access vector and full scope across other subdomains remain unclear.

title: Governed Individuation: Cryptographically Decoupling an Agent's Learning from Its Authority
blurb: This paper proposes "governed individuation," an architecture that confines a learning agent by binding it at boot to a cryptographically frozen identity digest and routing every action through a gate defined by its semantic effect rather than its name. The authors prove no amount of learning can widen the agent's authority without an operator-signed identity change. In a tool-use benchmark, effect-tracing gates cut false-allows from 75% under name-based gating to zero while preserving task success.

title: An AI agent ran 600 payloads and wrote its own ransom note in the JadePuffer intrusion, but the part Sysdig quietly admits changes what this attack actually proves about autonomous cybercrime
blurb: Sysdig documented JadePuffer, an AI agent that ran 600+ payloads and wrote its own ransom note, but a human handled setup, targeting, C2, and credentials.

title: Microsoft and SAP accelerate quantum security for enterprise
blurb: Microsoft and SAP both moved their enterprise software toward post-quantum cryptography, with Microsoft accelerating its Quantum Safe Program to transition all services and products to PQC by 2029 and SAP adding hybrid classical/quantum-resistant key exchange to SAP HANA Cloud's TLS 1.3 connections. Both cited harvest-now, decrypt-later attacks as a primary driver, in which adversaries steal encrypted data today to decrypt once cryptographically relevant quantum computers arrive.

title: ‘The risk to every organization has increased exponentially’: The FortiBleed campaign just took a turn for the worse
blurb: The FortiBleed campaign against internet-facing Fortinet VPNs and firewalls has now exposed privileged credentials for UK government and Foreign Office staff, some selling on the dark web for up to £40,000. SOCRadar attributes the operation to the Lynx/INC ransomware group and estimates over 70,000 devices compromised across 194 countries since last month, so operators running affected appliances at the edge should assume credential theft and rotate all VPN and privileged accounts.

title: Scaling Security Alert Triage With Specialized Agents on Databricks
blurb: Databricks rebuilt its low-severity alert triage around 17 source-specific agents, each tuned to a single detection source, after a single-prompt foundation model escalated 50% of alerts and produced only noise. Splitting the work by source gave each agent the false-positive patterns, behavioral baselines, and enrichment needed to triage accurately, with a shared threat-intelligence agent any triage agent can call for context. The design lets the IR team investigate LOW alerts they historically deprioritized rather than accepting the blind spots or adding headcount.

title: Your Scanner Doesn't Know What Time It Is
blurb: Cloud security scanners that evaluate configuration snapshots treat separately-collected observations as a single consistent state, so a compound finding like "this IAM role can reach 47 buckets" splices readings captured minutes apart into a claim about a system state that may never have existed. The author argues this is the normal operating condition, not an edge case, since collection windows span minutes to hours while infrastructure changes continuously. Treat cross-resource findings as unverified until you confirm the underlying snapshots are causally consistent.

title: TACTIC-KG: Toward Small Agent Teams for Cyber Threat Intelligence Knowledge Graph Construction
blurb: This paper introduces TACTIC-KG, an agentic framework that builds cybersecurity knowledge graphs from unstructured CTI reports by splitting the work across specialized lightweight LLM agents for extraction, typing, verification, and curation. Using 3B-8B models instead of a single large LLM, the authors report higher extraction F1, typing accuracy, and graph consistency at lower cost. For threat-intel teams, it's a case for decomposed small-model pipelines over monolithic LLMs when structuring adversary entities and relations at scale.

title: Knowledge Base Poisoning Attacks and Defense for Policy-Aware LLM-RAG Framework
blurb: This paper introduces Query-Agnostic Semantic Retrieval Poisoning, an attack that injects crafted rules into the knowledge base of a Policy-Aware LLM-RAG framework for battlefield IoT mission control, corrupting 85% of LLM context from a single rule at a 1.6% poisoning rate. The authors counter it with CLD-KB, a dual-detector anomaly framework that flags poisoned rules with 7ms overhead per mission.

title: The Privatization of Vulnerability Management
blurb: AI-driven vulnerability discovery is pushing detection, investigation, and response toward private vendors, as frontier models get gatekept to enterprise, the NVD's enrichment collapses, and "supply chain firewalls" host proprietary forks of open source repos. The author argues open source is most exposed, since its code is fully available for analysis while maintainers drown in AI-generated bug reports. The practical takeaway is that the data and tooling defenders rely on are increasingly paywalled behind vendors like Flashpoint, Vulncheck, and GitHub Security Advisories.

title: Moody Bible Institute breach leaves 2.3M accounts needing salvation, says cyber expert
blurb: A breach at Moody Bible Institute exposed roughly 2.3M accounts, according to a security researcher who flagged the exposed data. Affected users should treat their credentials as compromised, reset passwords, and watch for phishing that leverages the leaked account details, since religious and educational institutions remain frequent targets for low-effort data theft.

title: BeyondTrust warns of critical flaws in remote access software
blurb: BeyondTrust warned customers to patch two critical authentication-bypass flaws (CVE-2026-40138 and CVE-2026-40139) in its Remote Support and Privileged Remote Access products, which let unauthenticated attackers reach appliances and elevated accounts under certain configurations. Cloud instances were patched by April 21, but self-hosted customers must apply the rollup or upgrade to 25.3.3, with Shadowserver tracking nearly 2,000 exposed instances online.

title: Max severity Adobe ColdFusion flaw now exploited in attacks
blurb: Attackers are exploiting a maximum-severity Adobe ColdFusion vulnerability, CVE-2026-48282, that lets unauthenticated actors gain remote code execution on unpatched systems running versions 2025.9, 2023.20, and earlier. KEVIntel recorded in-the-wild exploitation within two hours of Adobe's Tuesday disclosure, and with Shadowserver tracking nearly 800 exposed instances online, admins should apply the patches immediately.

title: Linux Kernel Vulnerability Allows VM Escape on Intel and AMD Systems
blurb: Security researchers disclosed CVE-2026-53359, a use-after-free flaw in the KVM hypervisor's shadow MMU code, dubbed Januscape, that lets an attacker with root on a guest VM escape to the host and run code as root or crash every tenant on the same machine. It is the first KVM VM-escape known to work on both Intel and AMD, sat dormant for 16 years, and was patched in the mainline kernel on June 19.

title: BeyondTrust Patches Critical Auth Bypass Flaws in Remote Support and PRA
blurb: BeyondTrust patched four vulnerabilities in Remote Support and Privileged Remote Access, including two critical pre-auth flaws (CVE-2026-40138 and CVE-2026-40139, both CVSS 9.2) that let unauthenticated attackers bypass authentication and gain privileged access to appliances under specific configurations. Enterprises running these privileged-access gateways should patch immediately, since the products sit at the center of remote-admin trust and BeyondTrust found the bugs internally using AI models including Anthropic Claude Opus 4.8.

title: CERT/CC Warns of Hidden Admin Backdoor in Tenda Router Firmware
blurb: CERT/CC warned that multiple Tenda router firmware versions ship with an undocumented authentication backdoor, tracked as CVE-2026-11405, that grants full admin access to the web management interface without valid credentials. The flaw remains unpatched, so enterprises running affected AC and W-series Tenda hardware should disable remote management and restrict LAN reachability until a fix ships.

title: New Januscape Linux flaw allows VM escape on Intel, AMD devices
blurb: Researchers disclosed Januscape (CVE-2026-53359), a 16-year-old use-after-free flaw in KVM/x86's shadow MMU that lets an attacker with root in a guest VM escape to the host and run code across every tenant on the same server. The bug hits both Intel and AMD hosts and was patched in June 2026, so multi-tenant KVM operators should confirm commit 81ccda30b4e8 is applied.

title: Fake IT bods on Microsoft Teams coax workers into installing malware
blurb: Attackers are impersonating internal IT staff over Microsoft Teams to social-engineer employees into installing remote-access malware, exploiting the platform's default that lets external tenants message users directly. IT leaders should audit Teams external-access and federation settings, since the attack sidesteps email filtering entirely and lands in a channel most staff trust as an internal help-desk line.

title: Fake IT support calls on Microsoft Teams push EtherRAT malware
blurb: Attackers are impersonating corporate IT support over Microsoft Teams voice calls to trick employees into granting remote control and installing EtherRAT, a Node.js remote-access trojan that hands over full system control, per Palo Alto Networks' Unit 42. The campaign chains phishing emails, legitimate tools like AnyDesk and HopToDesk, and Ethereum smart contracts for resilient C2, and follows a wave of Teams-based intrusions pushing Microsoft to add new tenant-level protections.

title: Suspected China-Aligned Hackers Exploit Roundcube Flaws Against Universities
blurb: Suspected China-aligned hackers, tracked by Proofpoint as UNK_MassTraction, are exploiting patched Roundcube webmail flaws including CVE-2024-42009 to steal credentials, 2FA tokens, and cookies from physics and engineering departments at U.S. and Canadian universities. The XSS exploit triggers when a target opens a phishing email in Roundcube, letting attackers deploy web shells or the VShell tool and pivot into networks running unpatched N-day versions.

title: Suspected Chinese espionage group used a Roundcube exploit chain to burrow into universities
blurb: Proofpoint attributed a China-aligned espionage campaign, tracked as UNK_MassTraction, that chained two Roundcube vulnerabilities (CVE-2024-42009 and CVE-2025-49113) to steal credentials and plant webshells and backdoors inside U.S. and Canadian university physics and engineering departments. The zero-click chain triggers when a target opens a phishing email, marking a shift from the edge-device exploits China-aligned groups have long used and putting any organization running self-hosted Roundcube on notice to patch.

title: France to Stop Certifying Non-Quantum-Safe Encryption
blurb: France's cybersecurity agency ANSSI will stop certifying security products that lack quantum-resistant encryption starting in 2027, and told businesses to buy only quantum-safe products by 2030. Because ANSSI approval is mandatory for French government agencies and critical infrastructure, the policy is a de facto phase-out that forces vendors selling into that market to ship post-quantum cryptography or lose eligibility.

title: Nvidia pushes Kyber release to 2028 following manufacturing concerns: report
blurb: Nvidia delayed its Kyber NVL144 rack architecture to 2028, more than a year past its 2027 target, after PCB midplane manufacturing problems and a scrapped back-to-back Oberon workaround, SemiAnalysis reported. The slip leaves hyperscalers without a proven way to scale up Rubin Ultra clusters and likely pushes out the related NVL576 system, complicating data-center buildout roadmaps that assumed 2027 availability of the 600kW liquid-cooled racks.

title: CitrixBleed-ing Again? NetScaler Vulnerability Under Attack
blurb: Citrix disclosed CVE-2026-8451 on June 30, a high-severity memory-overread flaw (CVSS 8.8) in NetScaler ADC and Gateway devices configured as SAML identity providers, and attackers began exploiting it within 24 hours of WatchTowr publishing a proof-of-concept. Enterprises running NetScaler as an IDP should patch immediately, since the bug echoes 2023's CitrixBleed and can leak session data straight from appliance memory.

title: Iran-Linked Hackers Use New Cavern C2 Framework to Target Israeli Organizations
blurb: Check Point Research documented a previously undocumented modular C2 framework called Cavern, wielded by an Iran MOIS-linked group tracked as Cavern Manticore against Israeli IT providers and government organizations. The attack chain abuses SysAid's software update feature to side-load a trojanized DLL, then pulls post-exploitation modules over HTTPS for Active Directory recon, database theft, and SMB tunneling, putting enterprise IT-management tooling squarely in the supply-chain crosshairs.

title: ECB Asks Banks for Plans to Address AI Cybersecurity Threats
blurb: The European Central Bank asked banks to submit plans for defending against cybersecurity threats from frontier AI models, citing systems like Anthropic's Claude Mythos among the risks regulators now expect institutions to account for. The request signals that AI-driven attack capability is becoming a supervisory compliance item, pushing banks to fold model-aware threat assessments into existing security and risk-reporting obligations.

title: IBM debuts compact z17 mainframes and LinuxONE servers for on-premises enterprise AI
blurb: IBM released smaller z17 mainframes in a 19-inch rack-mount form factor and lower-priced LinuxONE 5 Express and Rockhopper 5 servers, targeting regulated enterprises that need to run AI workloads on-premises. The compact z17 packs up to 82 cores and 18TB of memory on the Telum II processor, while IBM pitches Rockhopper 5 as replacing 23 x86 servers with up to 83% lower power draw.

title: Microsoft wants to keep your AI agents from going rogue
blurb: Microsoft introduced Microsoft Execution Containers (MXC), a policy-driven runtime layer that sandboxes AI agents on Windows and WSL, now in early preview with process and session isolation. Agent 365 integrates the SDK natively and uses Entra and Intune to enforce per-agent constraints, letting IT teams apply least-privilege controls, audit agent activity, and match isolation to each workload's risk.

title: Spain collars alleged pro-Russia hacktivist after FBI tip-off
blurb: Spanish police arrested an alleged pro-Russia hacktivist in Palencia following a tip-off from the FBI, part of a widening cross-border effort against politically motivated cyberattacks on European targets. The arrest signals that Western law enforcement is coordinating more tightly on hacktivist attribution, raising the operational risk for enterprises running critical infrastructure that these groups increasingly target.

title: Microsoft lays off nearly 5,000 employees across Xbox, commercial sales
blurb: Microsoft cut roughly 4,800 roles, or 2.1% of its workforce, hitting Xbox hardest with 1,600 jobs gone and 3,200 total cuts expected through fiscal 2027. The company framed the restructuring around shifting AI-driven workloads while pouring $2.5B into its new Frontier Company unit for enterprise AI deployments, underscoring a pattern of job cuts tracking rising AI spend.

title: Measuring Healthcare Data Leaks and Security Flaws at Internet Scale
blurb: Researchers ran nine months of medical-protocol honeypots and internet-wide scans of DICOM, HL7, and FHIR services, finding widespread authentication flaws, missing transport encryption, and known software vulnerabilities with CVSS scores up to 9.8, including some exposing systems to full takeover. The study argues HL7 and FHIR remain badly underexamined despite carrying patient data, a gap IT teams running healthcare integrations should audit against their own exposed endpoints.

title: Tencent's Apache-licensed Hy3 takes on GLM-5.2 at half the size — and wins everywhere except coding
blurb: Tencent's Hunyuan team released the full version of Hy3, a 295B-parameter Mixture-of-Experts model with 21B active parameters, under the permissive Apache 2.0 license that lifts the EU, UK, and South Korea deployment restrictions blocking many rival Chinese open-weight models. The license change unblocks enterprises serving those regions, and Hy3 beats GLM-5.2 at half the size on most benchmarks, though it trails on coding.

title: thundersnap 0.01: an undo button for everything
blurb: Apenwarr released thundersnap 0.01, an open-source tool that replicates, forks, and shares container snapshots across cloud and personal hardware, using Tailscale to mesh bare-metal, Hetzner, and OVH machines into a single fabric with fast local disk and rollback. The pitch reframes ephemeral containers as the wrong primitive for both humans and AI agents, favoring portable snapshots that move to whichever provider offers the best hardware at the best price.

title: Microsoft to enable Windows settings backup by default for orgs
blurb: Microsoft will switch the Windows settings backup policy from disabled to enabled by default on Entra-joined and Entra hybrid-joined systems running Windows 11 26H2, excluding EU DMA-regulated regions and sovereign cloud environments. Admins keep full control through Intune or Group Policy, where explicit enable or disable settings override the default, and restore behavior still requires manual configuration.

title: Introducing Amazon Bedrock Managed Knowledge Base for faster, more accurate enterprise AI applications
blurb: AWS released Amazon Bedrock Managed Knowledge Base, a managed primitive that abstracts the full RAG stack—storage, embeddings, re-ranking, and foundation model selection—plus six pre-built connectors that pull data and permissions from SaaS sources. Enterprise teams already on Bedrock can stand up agentic AI applications against proprietary data without building or maintaining custom RAG pipelines, removing a procurement and infrastructure dependency on standalone vector and retrieval vendors.

title: Microsoft testing new Cloud Rebuild Windows 11 recovery feature
blurb: Microsoft began testing Cloud Rebuild in Windows 11 Insider Experimental builds, a recovery option that performs a full OS reinstall from Windows Update, pulling both the target image and device drivers so a machine returns to a known-good state even when it won't boot. It joins Point-in-Time Restore and Quick Machine Recovery under Microsoft's Windows Resiliency Initiative, cutting the need for USB media, custom images, or on-site IT access to recover fleet devices.

title: MPs tell Brit government: Sort out your tech sovereignty or get left out in the cold
blurb: A UK parliamentary committee warned the government that its dependence on US and Chinese hyperscalers leaves critical public-sector systems exposed and urged a coordinated push on domestic cloud, compute, and AI capacity. The report puts procurement pressure on Whitehall to favor sovereign and European providers, a shift that could reshape framework agreements currently dominated by Microsoft, AWS, and Google Cloud.

title: Microsoft cuts 4,800 jobs, as Xbox unit downsizes and plans to spin off four gaming studios
blurb: Microsoft is eliminating 4,800 jobs, 2.1% of its workforce, with the Xbox division absorbing 3,200 cuts and spinning off four studios including Double Fine and Ninja Theory. The move follows a 19% stock slide in 2026 as investors question Microsoft's AI payoff, and it signals a retreat from gaming to concentrate capital on cloud and AI infrastructure.

title: SAP’s Senior Reshuffle Follows AI-Induced Stock Rout
blurb: SAP replaced senior leadership after its stock fell 46% under CEO Christian Klein, as investors punished the slow payoff from its AI pivot and cloud-migration push. The reshuffle signals pressure on SAP to prove its Business AI and RISE with SAP bets can defend margins against a broader repricing of enterprise-software valuations.

title: Keyfactor Scores $1 Billion+ Investment for AI, Post-Quantum Security
blurb: Keyfactor raised a strategic growth investment exceeding $1B, led by Summit Partners, to expand its Trust Control Plane platform for managing machine and AI-agent identities across cloud, hybrid, and on-premises environments. The round follows the White House's June 2026 executive order mandating a federal move to quantum-safe cryptography by 2030, positioning Keyfactor's certificate-lifecycle and post-quantum tooling against fragmented point solutions as machine identities outnumber human ones.

title: Microsoft betting that enterprise AI needs engineers, not bigger sales teams
blurb: Microsoft cut roughly 4,800 jobs this week, about 2.1% of its workforce, mostly in commercial sales and Xbox, days after launching Frontier Company to embed its engineers alongside customers deploying AI. The move signals Microsoft is betting on engineering expertise over traditional account management for AI adoption, which reshapes how CIOs get implementation support and pressures the systems integrators that once owned that work.

title: Google hobbles NetNut proxy infrastructure amid botnet crackdown
blurb: Google, working with Lumen and the FBI, disrupted NetNut, one of the largest residential proxy networks at an estimated two million devices, by disabling the Google accounts and services it used for malware command and control. The takedown caused "significant degradation" and cut the available device pool by millions, extending Google's ongoing campaign against malicious proxy networks after its January action against IPIDEA.

title: Meta: Recycling memory is magic solution to RAM supply misery
blurb: Meta detailed Vistara, a CXL-based ASIC that lets decommissioned DDR4 modules serve as expanded memory in DDR5 servers, addressing the roughly 40% of its fleet that is memory-capacity bound amid AI-driven RAM shortages. Meta reports up to a 25% reduction in server count for disaggregated ML inference, 29% lower average latency for distributed caches, and 33% fewer job failures, giving hyperscalers a real-world validation of CXL for memory reuse.

title: Microsoft scrambles to secure AI talent, guts Xbox workforce
blurb: Microsoft is redeploying 6,000 industry and engineering experts under its new Frontier Company initiative while cutting Xbox headcount to fund the AI push. The reshuffle signals that enterprise buyers should expect Microsoft to prioritize Copilot and agent-platform engineering over consumer gaming, concentrating its roadmap investment where it sees enterprise revenue.

title: Join video conferences on Google Meet hardware via SIP through Pexip
blurb: Google Meet hardware can now join meetings on any SIP-compatible platform through a Pexip interop gateway, including DTMF controls for navigating IVR prompts and entering numeric passcodes, on both Android and ChromeOS room devices. The feature is off by default and available to all Workspace customers with Meet hardware, though it requires a separate Pexip subscription and rolls out gradually starting July 6.

title: Google has introduced new rules for Chrome extensions
blurb: Google updated its Chrome Web Store Developer Program Policies to tighten extension data collection, requiring that any data gathered be strictly necessary to an extension's single disclosed purpose and that all collection be prominently disclosed, including any changes made after install. The update also bans extensions that circumvent AI-service safety guardrails and prohibits prediction-market tools, with enforcement beginning August 1, 2026.

title: Microsoft Can Track Users via a Windows Device ID
blurb: A hacker's arrest revealed that Microsoft can link a Windows-generated device ID to an individual user, tying hardware installations back to Microsoft accounts and enabling identification across devices. The disclosure sharpens telemetry and privacy questions for enterprise fleets running Windows, where IT teams standardizing on Microsoft accounts now have documented evidence of how deeply device identity ties into user tracking.

title: Zscaler finds autonomous agents succumb to IPI traps
blurb: Zscaler tested 26 LLMs against indirect prompt injection (IPI) attacks that hide malicious instructions in web content, and found several autonomous agents fell for scams a human would catch, with four models including Gemini-2.5-pro and two Llama 3 variants flagged as vulnerable. Critics counter that Zscaler's binary safe/vulnerable labels oversimplify agent risk, which shifts as models ingest new data, making point-in-time results unreliable guidance for CISOs.

title: Samsung says memory chip demand will drive 19-fold jump in operating profit
blurb: Samsung projected a 19-fold jump in Q2 operating profit to a record 89.4 trillion won (about $58.47B), driven by record memory-chip prices and AI-server demand for server DRAM. The forecast beat consensus and eased analyst concerns about AI-spending sustainability, signaling that enterprises building AI infrastructure should plan for sustained upward pressure on memory pricing and procurement costs.

title: Gemini Code Assist will shut down on July 17
blurb: Google is shutting down the consumer version of Gemini Code Assist on GitHub, ending all automated code review activity from the app on July 17, 2026, with new installations already blocked as of June 18. The enterprise version is unaffected, so business customers keep their agentic code review while individual developers on the free tier lose it and must uninstall the GitHub app.

title: HCP Terraform Powered by Infragraph Limited Availability Launch
blurb: HashiCorp launched HCP Terraform powered by Infragraph into limited availability for Standard and Premium customers on US instances, adding a live graph view that maps resources, relationships, and state across environments. The release expands connectors to Azure, GitHub, and Terraform Enterprise alongside AWS, giving platform teams a unified inventory to track resource ownership, surface risk, and prioritize security patching without stitching together separate tools.

title: IBM teases new rackable mainframes that ‘complete’ the z17 family
blurb: IBM previewed rackable versions of its z17 mainframes, extending the line into standard 19-inch data center racks and filling out the family it launched earlier this year. The rack form factor lowers the deployment barrier for enterprises that want mainframe-class transaction and AI-inference capacity without the footprint and facilities requirements of traditional standalone z-series systems.

title: Microsoft says the world is changing faster than it can keep up as it guts commercial, Xbox teams
blurb: Microsoft is cutting staff across its commercial and Xbox divisions, with Xbox chief Sarah Bond warning that the company can't mistake longevity for inevitability as the market shifts faster than it can adapt. The layoffs signal Microsoft reprioritizing headcount toward AI infrastructure and cloud, a reallocation enterprise buyers should read as continued pressure on the pace and support of its non-AI product lines.

title: EU urged to act after Pegasus infects phone of spyware inquiry MEP
blurb: The phone of an MEP serving on the EU's spyware inquiry committee was infected with NSO Group's Pegasus, prompting renewed calls for the bloc to impose binding limits on commercial spyware. The breach of an official investigating surveillance abuse strengthens the case for stricter EU rules, which would tighten mobile-device security obligations and procurement scrutiny for vendors selling into European public-sector and enterprise environments.

title: ‘The risk to every organization has increased exponentially’: The FortiBleed campaign just took a turn for the worse
blurb: The FortiBleed credential-harvesting campaign escalated after The Telegraph reported that exposed logins now include privileged Fortinet credentials for UK Foreign Office and government staff, with some accounts selling on the dark web for up to £40,000. SOCRadar attributed the campaign to the Lynx/INC ransomware group and Arctic Wolf traced it to an automated credential factory hitting more than 70,000 internet-facing Fortinet VPNs and firewalls across 194 countries.

title: With AI, a wrong answer is a bug. A wrong action is an incident
blurb: The author argues that banking AI has crossed from reading and summarizing into taking autonomous actions, which turns a wrong answer from a quality bug into a reportable incident and makes overprivileged agents the leading security risk. The Five Eyes cyber agencies named privilege the top agentic risk in their April 2026 joint guidance and called strict least privilege critical. For CISOs, that shifts least-privilege scoping of agents from best practice to expected practice on a compressed timeline.

title: Apple adds new popup for AI features that send data to Google Cloud
blurb: Apple added a permission popup in iOS 26 and iOS 27 that notifies users when AI features route their data to Google Cloud, extending Private Cloud Compute beyond Apple's own silicon for the first time. Apple says the Google Cloud deployment reuses the same isolation patterns as PCC on Apple silicon, but the move breaks the on-Apple-hardware guarantee that security researchers praised at Apple Intelligence's 2024 launch.

title: Microsoft's layoffs extend beyond Xbox
blurb: Microsoft cut 3,200 jobs across its Commercial Business and other divisions today, on top of Xbox-arm layoffs that bring the day's total to 4,800 people, or 2.1% of its global workforce. Chief People Officer Amy Coleman said the eliminated roles are not being replaced by AI but signaled that automation is already reshaping the work, framing the cuts as part of a broader restructuring rather than a one-off.

title: AWS Weekly Roundup: Claude Sonnet 5 on AWS, Amazon WorkSpaces for AI agents, AWS service availability updates, and more (July 6, 2026)
blurb: AWS made Anthropic's Claude Sonnet 5 available on Bedrock and shipped a batch of enterprise updates, including Amazon WorkSpaces for AI agents, EC2 C9g instances on Graviton5, a CloudFormation Express mode, and EKS Kubernetes version rollbacks. Enterprise teams standardized on AWS get frontier Anthropic models and agent tooling inside their existing procurement, while the Graviton5 and rollback features cut compute costs and de-risk cluster upgrades.

title: Microsoft and SAP accelerate quantum security for enterprise
blurb: Microsoft accelerated its Quantum Safe Program to move all products and services to post-quantum cryptography by 2029, while partner SAP added PQC to HANA Cloud's TLS connections via a hybrid classical and quantum-resistant key exchange. Both moves target harvest-now, decrypt-later attacks, pushing enterprises to inventory where cryptography lives across legacy infrastructure and adopt crypto-agility before quantum decryption becomes viable.

title: AWS caps new users in checkmate for Mechanical Turk microtask platform
blurb: AWS closed Mechanical Turk to new customers effective July 30, 2026, and confirmed it has no plans for new features beyond security and availability maintenance, the standard prelude to a shutdown. The 2005 crowdsourced-labor marketplace has been undercut by agentic AI handling the data-annotation and microtask work it once routed to human workers, part of the same shift Gartner estimates could redirect $234B away from SaaS spending.

title: Agentic AI to disrupt $234B in SaaS spending: Gartner
blurb: Gartner projects that agentic AI will disrupt $234B in SaaS spending as AI agents replace traditional software interfaces, undercutting the per-seat licensing model that underpins most enterprise SaaS. For IT buyers, the shift threatens vendors whose value sits in dashboards and manual workflows rather than the underlying data and systems of record. The near-term question is whether incumbents like Salesforce and ServiceNow reprice around outcomes before agents route around their UIs entirely.

title: Broadcom and Apple extend custom silicon pact to 2031
blurb: Broadcom and Apple extended their custom silicon agreement through 2031, locking in Apple as a multiyear customer for Broadcom's wireless and networking chips as Apple continues to design more components in-house. The deal gives Broadcom revenue visibility on a marquee account through the decade and signals that Apple's silicon independence still leaves room for external suppliers on RF and connectivity parts.

title: Samsung’s profits jump 19x in a year and you don’t need AI to figure out why
blurb: Samsung reported operating profit roughly 19 times higher than a year earlier, driven almost entirely by soaring DRAM and HBM memory prices as AI datacenter buildout outstrips supply. The same demand lifting Samsung's margins is pushing up server and storage costs for enterprise buyers, who now face tighter memory availability and higher pricing on procurement cycles through the rest of the year.

title: Samsung floats 2028 launch for seaborne datacenter
blurb: Samsung proposed a floating, seaborne datacenter targeting a 2028 launch, using ocean water for cooling and potentially offshore power to sidestep the land, grid, and permitting constraints slowing terrestrial buildouts. The concept is early and unproven at scale, but it signals how far hyperscale operators and their suppliers will go to secure cooling and power capacity as AI demand outpaces conventional datacenter siting.

title: Alibaba 'bans' Anthropic in AI cold war as cybersecurity regulation heats up in China
blurb: Alibaba reportedly banned employees from using Anthropic's Claude models, citing an alleged backdoor, and directed staff to its in-house Qoder coding platform instead. The move escalates a US-China AI standoff that saw Anthropic accuse Alibaba of running nearly 29 million fraudulent-account exchanges to distill Claude, and it lands as China's revamped Cybersecurity Law tightens scrutiny of foreign LLM use.

title: Microsoft finally fixes one of the biggest headaches of reinstalling Windows
blurb: Microsoft is adding automatic driver recovery to Windows reinstalls, letting the OS pull the correct drivers during a clean install instead of leaving users to hunt them down afterward. For IT teams that reimage fleets, this cuts the manual driver-chasing that follows every wipe, reducing post-reinstall ticket volume and shortening the time to return a machine to a working state.

title: 시스코, 사내 AI 비서로 ‘섀도 AI’ 차단…직원당 주 5~6시간 업무 절감
blurb: Cisco curbed shadow AI by building an internal multi-model assistant that unifies coding help, HR tasks, and RAG search over company documents, reaching 96,000 employees and 90% adoption while saving engineers about six hours a week and other staff five. At roughly $10 per user monthly, Cisco says the platform runs cheaper than commercial assistant subscriptions, giving enterprises a build-versus-buy data point on consolidating sanctioned AI access rather than blocking it.

title: NTT evolves distributed data centers with photonics drive
blurb: NTT DoCoMo launched a "GPU over APN" testbed that distributes GPUs across eight Japanese cities and links them over its all-photonic IOWN network, hitting 100 Gb/s RDMA connectivity and moving 25GB between Fukuoka and Tokyo in two seconds. The approach lets NTT spread AI training and inference across smaller renewable-powered facilities instead of centralized data centers, and pairs with a new $500M IOWN-AI fund backed by Chunghwa Telecom, SK Group, and the Development Bank of Japan.

title: Chinese lidar maker with Nvidia ties accused of being cyber risk for U.S.
blurb: Hesai Technology, a Shanghai-based lidar maker the Pentagon blacklisted as a Chinese military entity in 2024, is expanding its U.S. footprint through a partnership that makes its sensors an option on Nvidia's autonomous-vehicle platforms. The blacklist bars Pentagon contracts but not commercial use, and security officials warn that Chinese lidar embedded in cars, robots, and factory equipment could hand Beijing a backdoor to sensitive infrastructure data.

title: Government's cyber pledge lands 60 signatories, including M&S and, somehow, Capita
blurb: The UK government's Cyber Security Charter drew 60 corporate signatories pledging to adopt baseline security practices, including M&S, whose 2025 breach cost it roughly £300M, and outsourcer Capita, itself fined £14M over a 2023 breach. The voluntary charter carries no enforcement mechanism, so procurement teams evaluating these vendors should treat the pledge as a signal of intent rather than a guarantee of hardened security posture.

title: Northern Ireland tries (again) to expel Capita from schools IT contract
blurb: Northern Ireland's Education Authority has moved again to terminate its schools IT contract with Capita, reviving a dispute over the outsourcer's delivery on the managed-service deal. The renewed push signals continued procurement risk for public-sector buyers tied to Capita, whose large government contracts have repeatedly drawn scrutiny over performance and cost.

title: Microsoft confirms an upcoming Windows 11 26H2 default change
blurb: Microsoft confirmed a default configuration change coming in Windows 11 26H2, now rolling out to Insider channels for testing ahead of general availability. IT admins managing Windows fleets should track the 26H2 Insider builds now to catch the new default before it ships, since defaults that change silently can shift device behavior across managed endpoints without an explicit policy update.

title: Your Worker can now have its own cache in front of it
blurb: Cloudflare launched Workers Cache, a tiered cache that sits in front of any Worker, enabled with a single line of Wrangler config and driven by the standard Cache-Control and Cache-Tag headers you already use. Cache hits skip Worker execution entirely, so you stop paying CPU time for repeat requests, and per-entrypoint control lets you slot cache stages anywhere in a chain of internal entrypoints, not just the public one.

title: Threat Actors Probe Gitea Docker Flaw CVE-2026-20896 13 Days After Disclosure
blurb: Sysdig detected the first in-the-wild exploitation attempt against CVE-2026-20896, a CVSS 9.8 flaw in Gitea's official Docker images that hard-code REVERSE_PROXY_TRUSTED_PROXIES to a wildcard, letting any client that reaches the container spoof an X-WEBAUTH-USER header and authenticate as admin. Gitea 1.26.3 removes the wildcard and makes reverse-proxy auth opt-in, but roughly 6,200 internet-facing instances remain exposed 13 days after disclosure.

title: New Januscape Linux flaw allows VM escape on Intel, AMD devices
blurb: Security researcher Hyunwoo Kim disclosed Januscape (CVE-2026-53359), a use-after-free bug in KVM/x86 shadow MMU emulation that lets an attacker with root inside a guest VM execute code on the host or panic the kernel, taking down every co-tenant VM on the same machine. It is the first guest-to-host escape that triggers on both Intel and AMD, and multi-tenant operators should confirm patch commit 81ccda30b4e8 is applied to their host kernels.

title: See your serverless: introducing the Headlamp plugin for Knative
blurb: The Knative SIG released a Headlamp plugin that consolidates KService management, traffic splitting, autoscaling config, and resource mapping into a single UI, replacing the constant jumping between the kn CLI, kubectl, and the dashboard. Operators can edit traffic splits for canary rollouts, restart pods, and view logs inline, with every action gated by the user's existing RBAC permissions.

title: Apple Container: Open-source tool for Linux containers on the Mac
blurb: Apple released Container 1.0.0, an open-source Swift tool that runs each Linux workload in its own lightweight VM on Apple silicon Macs and works with any OCI-compatible registry. The release adds long-lived container machine environments and a container cp command, but moves config to a TOML file and drops the v0 XPC interface, both breaking changes that require macOS 26 and updated scripts.

title: Meta rebuilds its AI storage stack from the ground up to stop GPUs sitting idle
blurb: Meta rebuilt the BLOB storage layer on top of its Tectonic fabric to keep AI training GPUs fed, cutting cross-region data-move times by up to 97%. The engineers collapsed fragmented metadata into a single ZippyDB-backed schema for near-instant path lookups, replaced the dataplane proxy with a fat client that streams bytes straight from storage servers, and colocated storage regionally with the GPUs that consume it. Meta claims the redesign added zero overhead on Tectonic while staying inside its power budget.

title: 16-Year-Old Linux KVM Flaw Lets Guest VMs Escape to Host on Intel and AMD x86 Systems
blurb: A 16-year-old use-after-free bug in Linux KVM's shadow MMU, dubbed Januscape (CVE-2026-53359), lets a guest VM with root corrupt host kernel memory on both Intel and AMD, the first known guest-to-host escape working on both. The public proof-of-concept panics the host and takes every co-tenant VM down; a fix landed in mainline on June 19, and cloud operators should prioritize patching multi-tenant hosts.

title: Linux Kernel Vulnerability Allows VM Escape on Intel and AMD Systems
blurb: Security researcher Hyunwoo Kim disclosed Januscape (CVE-2026-53359), a use-after-free bug in KVM's shadow MMU that allows guest-to-host VM escape on both Intel and AMD, the first KVM exploit to work on both architectures. It threatens multi-tenant public clouds running untrusted guests, letting a single rented instance panic the host or gain root RCE over every co-located tenant; it was patched in mainline on June 19 after 16 years dormant.

title: Can LLMs Really Recover Microservice Failures? A Recovery-Aware Evaluation of Diagnosis-to-Action Reasoning
blurb: This paper introduces R2Act, a benchmark of 302 quality-audited Kubernetes incidents that evaluates whether LLMs can move from diagnosis to a valid recovery action, not just name a root cause. The strongest RAG-based models hit 91.4%–99.7% root-cause accuracy but only 36.8%–60.3% recovery validity, and they pick invalid actions for up to 62% of incidents they diagnosed correctly. The gap sits in translating evidence into an admissible fix, which is the part that matters for autonomous incident response.

title: Proof-of-Concept Exploit Released for Linux ‘Bad Epoll’ Root Access Vulnerability
blurb: A proof-of-concept exploit is now public for Bad Epoll (CVE-2026-46242), a use-after-free race condition in the Linux kernel's epoll facility that lets unprivileged processes escalate to root on distros running kernel 6.4 or newer, including Pixel 10 phones. The PoC leaks kernel memory and hijacks an indirect call via a ROP chain, and a correct maintainer patch landed only two months after the initial report.

title: Automatic Upgrades: best practice features for your lakehouse tables
blurb: Databricks released Auto Upgrades for Unity Catalog managed tables, which observes client access patterns over a rolling window, verifies workload compatibility, and then applies best-practice features like Row Tracking via a background ALTER TABLE job with no manual effort. Every change is reversible per table, and once all existing tables in a schema pass verification the feature becomes a default that new tables inherit automatically.

title: thundersnap 0.01: an undo button for everything
blurb: Apenwarr released thundersnap 0.01, an open-source tool that makes container snapshots fast to replicate, fork, share, and run across cloud providers and personal hardware, using Tailscale to knit bare-metal boxes and rented Hetzner or OVH nodes into a single mesh. It pitches persistent snapshots with an undo button as a better substrate than ephemeral containers for both humans and AI agents who want unlimited disk, fast CPUs, and provider portability.

title: Meta: Recycling memory is magic solution to RAM supply misery
blurb: Meta detailed Vistara, a CXL-based ASIC that exposes DDR4 modules pulled from decommissioned servers as an extra NUMA node on newer DDR5 hosts, letting memory-capacity-bound machines reuse RAM that outlives the servers it shipped in. In Meta's numbers the bridge cut server count by up to 25% for disaggregated ML inference, dropped distributed-cache latency 29%, and reduced job-failure frequency 33%, validating CXL against the AI-driven memory crunch.

title: Reduce CDN log costs with searchable archives
blurb: The Datadog post lays out a pattern for retaining high-volume CDN edge logs without indexing every event, which at streaming scale can reach tens of terabytes per day. Observability Pipelines routes raw logs to object storage while forwarding high-signal events for alerting, and Archive Search queries the archived data from Datadog rather than a separate S3-plus-Athena environment. The trade-off it targets is the fragmentation of moving historical investigations into a second query language and access-control model.

title: Why sandboxing your agent is not enough
blurb: Two CNCF-adjacent projects tackle secure AI-agent execution on Kubernetes differently. Agent-sandbox, under SIG Apps, wraps agents in Sandbox CRDs with strong identities, persistent storage, and pod-lifecycle isolation. Agent-substrate goes further for scale: agents run in secure worker pods for short bursts, suspend when idle, and resume on any available worker, decoupling the agent actor from the pod and packing more agents onto the same infrastructure.

title: Beyond Compliance: A Large Scale Study on the Completeness and Consistency of the GitHub SBOMs
blurb: This paper evaluates GitHub's auto-generated SBOMs against Syft, Trivy, and the Microsoft SBOM Tool across 10,000 repositories spanning ten language ecosystems. GitHub's SBOMs fail NTIA compliance but reliably carry core metadata, and they match the Microsoft tool while often beating Syft and Trivy on version and license coverage. The catch for anyone using SBOMs for vulnerability or license analysis is that completeness swings hard by ecosystem, driven by how each language manages dependencies.

title: HCP Terraform Powered by Infragraph Limited Availability Launch
blurb: HashiCorp moved HCP Terraform powered by Infragraph into limited availability for Standard and Premium customers on US instances, adding Azure, GitHub, and Terraform Enterprise connectors alongside AWS to build a live graph of resources, relationships, and state. The unified view targets the multi-cloud data silos that make ownership, security patching, and cost tracking hard, letting platform teams query infrastructure instead of stitching inventories together by hand.

title: Migrating Counter Service storage: Design choices and learnings
blurb: Grab's anti-fraud team migrated its Counter Service, which handles ~1 billion requests per day at tens of thousands of QPS, off a wide-column store onto Aerospike while decoupling storage from business logic. The writeup covers the reader-side safety scaffolding built for a gradual rollout, a redesigned writer-side data model that collapsed the original four network round-trips per event, and the optimizations surfaced by revisiting access patterns. The decoupling is framed as the reusable payoff, cutting the effort for any future storage swap.

title: Event-driven plumbing. When plain SQS + Lambda beats EventBridge Pipes
blurb: The author argues that for SQS-to-Lambda-to-SQS enrichment, plain SQS plus Lambda beats EventBridge Pipes, which charge $0.40 per million requests and bill every 64KB payload chunk as a separate request. Pipes earn their keep for low-code HTTP API enrichment on an event stream, but when your enrichment is already a Lambda, you pay in both money and lost failure granularity for handling your serverless primitives do better. The post walks through a multi-million-document S3 enrichment job to make the case.

title: Your Scanner Doesn't Know What Time It Is
blurb: The author argues that cloud security scanners produce compound findings by splicing config snapshots captured minutes apart, asserting a system state that may never have existed at any single moment. A scanner reporting "this IAM role can reach 47 S3 buckets" can be wrong if the policy changed inside the collection window, and nothing flags the inconsistency. The post applies Lamport's 1978 causal-ordering work to argue scanners need consistent-snapshot semantics, not just point-in-time reads.

title: Configure Azure Pipelines app in ghe.com
blurb: A developer documenting how to install the Azure Pipelines GitHub app on ghe.com (GitHub Enterprise Managed Users with Data Residency), a step Azure DevOps's service-connection UI assumes exists but never explains. The fix is an undocumented URL, `https://{slug}.ghe.com/apps/external-app/azure-pipelines`, reverse-engineered from the Azure Boards app path after GitHub and Microsoft contacts came up empty. From there the service connection authorizes cleanly and pipelines rewire from Azure Repos to GitHub.

title: OpenSSH 10.4/10.4p1 Released
blurb: The OpenSSH project released 10.4/10.4p1, patching five security issues including SFTP downloads that could land files in unexpected locations, SCP remote-to-remote writes escaping to parent directories, and a pre-auth GSSAPI denial of service. The release also adds an opt-in post-quantum signature scheme combining ML-DSA-44 and Ed25519, plus stricter transport enforcement that disconnects peers sending non-key-exchange messages during rekey to curb memory exhaustion.

title: DNSGlobe – Rust TUI to watch DNS propagate around the world
blurb: DNSGlobe is a Rust terminal UI that queries 34 public DNS resolvers worldwide in parallel, compares their answers, and renders record propagation on a live world map with a watch mode that re-polls until every server agrees. Each resolver is queried directly with no cache, EDNS0, and TCP fallback, and anycast POPs like Cloudflare, Google, and Quad9 are probed to reveal which site is actually serving your queries.

title: Durable Workflows on AWS: Lambda Durable Functions, Step Functions, and MWAA
